```mermaid
    style C1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style D1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The blue boxes represent the **first (lowest) stage** that directly accepts raw data inputs. The purple boxes represent the **middle stage**. The green boxes represent the **final stage** whose output is the final Y. The orange/yellow boxes show how the selection word is partitioned.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*What is a multiplexer tree? Why is it needed in digital design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multiplexer tree is a hierarchical network formed by cascading two or more smaller multiplexer ICs in multiple stages to realize a single large multiplexer with greater fan-in than any single IC can provide.

It is needed because:
1. **(1 mark)** Commercial MUX ICs have limited fan-in (typically 2, 4, 8, or 16 inputs only).
2. **(1 mark)** Manufacturing large-fan-in MUXs in a single IC is impractical due to pin-count, internal-gate fan-in, and cost constraints.
3. **(1 mark)** MUX trees allow designers to build arbitrarily large multiplexers (e.g., 64-to-1, 128-to-1) using only standard, cheap, readily available small MUX ICs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**
*An 8-to-1 MUX has three select lines. How many select lines are required for a 64-to-1 MUX? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 64-to-1 MUX requires **n = log₂(64) = 6** selection lines.

**Justification (2 marks):** Each of the 64 input lines must be uniquely addressable. With n binary selection lines, we can generate 2ⁿ distinct combinations. To address 64 inputs, we need 2ⁿ ≥ 64, so n ≥ 6. The minimum value is n = 6.

---

---

### **PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]*

**(a)** Design a **32-to-1 multiplexer tree** using only **8-to-1 MUX ICs**. Show the complete block diagram, label all inputs, and specify the selection-line connections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the design in part (a), determine the **total number of MUX ICs required**, the **number of stages**, and write the **Boolean expression for the final output Y** in terms of the input lines and selection variables. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Selection-Line Mapping (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- N = 32, k = 8, m = log₂8 = 3, n = log₂32 = 5
- Selection word: **S₄ S₃ S₂ S₁ S₀**

**Step 2:** Number of stages.
- h = log₈(32) = 5/3 → Since this is not an integer, we round up: h = 2 stages.

**Step 3:** Partition the 32 inputs into 4 groups of 8 (handled by 4 first-stage MUXs):

| First-Stage MUX | Inputs Handled |
|-----------------|----------------|
| MUX-1A | I₀, I₁, I₂, I₃, I₄, I₅, I₆, I₇ |
| MUX-1B | I₈, I₉, I₁₀, I₁₁, I₁₂, I₁₃, I₁₄, I₁₅ |
| MUX-1C | I₁₆, I₁₇, I₁₈, I₁₉, I₂₀, I₂₁, I₂₂, I₂₃ |
| MUX-1D | I₂₄, I₂₅, I₂₆, I₂₇, I₂₈, I₂₉, I₃₀, I₃₁ |

**Step 4:** Second-stage MUX (one 8-to-1 MUX) selects among the 4 outputs of the first stage. Inputs 4–7 of the second-stage MUX are grounded.

**Step 5:** Selection-line connections:
- **First-stage MUXs** receive: **S₂, S₁, S₀** (LSBs)
- **Second-stage MUX** receives: **S₄, S₃** (MSBs)

**Block Diagram (text representation for answer sheet):**

```
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
        S₄ S₃ ─────►│  8-to-1 MUX (Stage 2)│──────► Y
                    └─────────────────────┘
                       ▲   ▲   ▲   ▲
                       │   │   │   │
        ┌──────────────┘   │   │   └──────────────┐
        │                  │   │                  │
   ┌────▼────┐        ┌────▼────┐        ┌────▼────┐
   │ 8-to-1  │        │ 8-to-1  │        │ 8-to-1  │   ...
   │ MUX-1A  │        │ MUX-1B  │        │ MUX-1C  │
   └────┬────┘        └────┬────┘        └────┬────┘
        │ Inputs I0..I7   │ Inputs I8..I15  │ Inputs I16..I23
        S₂S₁S₀            S₂S₁S₀            S₂S₁S₀
```

(Complete block diagram as shown in SECTION_4.1 should be drawn.)

**(b) Total ICs, Stages, and Boolean Expression (7 marks):**

**Total ICs:** 4 (first stage) + 1 (second stage) = **5 MUXs** ✓

**Number of stages:** **2** ✓

**Boolean expression:**
The output of the second-stage MUX:

$$
Y = \bar{S_4}\bar{S_3} Y_A + \bar{S_4} S_3 Y_B + S_4 \bar{S_3} Y_C + S_4 S_3 Y_D
$$

where $Y_A, Y_B, Y_C, Y_D$ are outputs of the first-stage MUXs. Expanding $Y_D$ (for I₂₄ to I₃₁):

$$
Y_D = \bar{S_2}\bar{S_1}\bar{S_0} I_{24} + \bar{S_2}\bar{S_1} S_0 I_{25} + \dots + S_2 S_1 S_0 I_{31}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
Y = \sum_{i=0}^{31} m_i(S_4, S_3, S_2, S_1, S_0) \cdot I_i
$$

where $m_i$ is the i-th minterm of the 5 selection variables — the standard MUX equation. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Set B]*

**(a)** Design a **64-to-1 multiplexer tree** using only **4-to-1 MUX ICs**. Show the complete multi-stage block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the design in (a) with an alternative design using **8-to-1 MUX ICs** in terms of **number of ICs, number of stages, propagation delay, and cost**. State which design is preferable and why. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 64-to-1 MUX Using 4-to-1 Sub-MUXs (7 marks):**

**Parameters:**
- N = 64, k = 4, m = 2, n = log₂64 = 6
- h = log₄(64) = 3 stages

**Stage-wise breakdown:**

| Stage | Number of MUXs | Function | Selection Lines |
|-------|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| Stage 1 (bottom) | 16 MUXs of 4-to-1 | Each handles 4 of the 64 inputs | S₁ S₀ (LSBs) |
| Stage 2 (middle) | 4 MUXs of 4-to-1 | Each combines 4 outputs of stage 1 | S₃ S₂ (mid bits) |
| Stage 3 (top) | 1 MUX of 4-to-1 | Final selection → Y | S₅ S₄ (MSBs) |

**Total MUXs = 16 + 4 + 1 = 21 MUXs** ✓

**Block Diagram:** As shown in SECTION_4.2.

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Design 1: 4-to-1 sub-MUXs | Design 2: 8-to-1 sub-MUXs |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Number of stages | **3** | **2** |
| Total MUX ICs | **21** | **9** |
| Total IC packages (74LS153 has 2 MUXs/chip; 74LS151 has 1) | 11 packages of 74LS153 | 9 packages of 74LS151 |
| Propagation delay (in units of t_pd per MUX) | 3 × t_pd = 3 t_pd | 2 × t_pd = 2 t_pd |
| Cost (assuming 74LS151 = ₹40, 74LS153 = ₹30) | 9 × 40 = ₹360 OR 11 × 30 = ₹330 | 9 × 40 = ₹360 |
| Design complexity (PCB routing) | Higher (more ICs) | Lower (fewer ICs) |

**Verdict (2 marks):** The **8-to-1 sub-MUX design is preferable** because:
1. It uses **fewer ICs** (9 vs 21).
2. It has **fewer stages** (2 vs 3), so **lower propagation delay** and **higher operating frequency**.
3. The PCB is **simpler to route** with fewer chips.

The trade-off is that 74LS151 ICs are slightly more expensive per chip, but the total system cost is comparable or lower because far fewer chips are needed. **Fewer stages also mean lower cumulative noise and better signal integrity.**

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to ground unused inputs (–1 to –2 marks):** When the number of MUX outputs in a stage is less than the input capacity of the next-stage MUX, the unused pins MUST be tied to a defined logic level. The examiner will deduct marks if this is omitted.
>
> 2. **Wrong selection-line partitioning (–2 marks):** The MSBs of the selection word MUST drive the higher-stage (output-side) MUX, and the LSBs MUST drive the lower-stage (input-side) MUX. Reversing this leads to incorrect input being selected. Always verify by tracing one specific selection.
>
> 3. **Not justifying the number of stages (–1 mark):** A common KTU demand is to *show* that h = log_k(N). Students often just state h without derivation.
>
> 4. **Confusing the geometric series formula applicability (–1 mark):** The formula (N–1)/(k–1) only works when N is a perfect power of k. Always check first.
>
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling selection lines (–2 marks):** Every MUX block in the diagram must clearly show which selection lines connect to which stage. A diagram without this labeling is considered incomplete.
>
> 6. **Miscounting total MUXs (–1 mark):** Re-count by adding stage-wise counts. Don't estimate.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multiplexer Trees**
>
> - [ ] A **Multiplexer (MUX)** is a data selector: 2ⁿ inputs, n select lines, 1 output.
> - [ ] A **MUX Tree** is a cascade of smaller MUXs used to implement a larger virtual MUX.
> - [ ] Key formula: **n = log₂(N)** for selection lines.
> - [ ] Key formula: **h = ⌈log_k(N)⌉** for number of stages.
> - [ ] Key formula (when N = kʰ): **Total MUXs = (N – 1) / (k – 1)**.
> - [ ] **Selection-line partitioning rule:** MSBs drive the upper (output-side) stages; LSBs drive the lower (input-side) stages.
> - [ ] Always **ground unused inputs** of the final-stage MUX.
> - [ ] **Fewer stages = lower propagation delay** → design preference for high-speed circuits.
> - [ ] **Fewer ICs = lower cost and simpler PCB** → design preference for compact systems.
> - [ ] Standard ICs: 74LS151 (8-to-1), 74LS153 (dual 4-to-1), 74LS157 (quad 2-to-1).
> - [ ] Always **verify your design** by tracing a specific input: e.g., S₄S₃S₂S₁S₀ = 11010 should select I₂₆.
> - [ ] MUX trees are foundational for **FPGA LUT design, ALU operand selection, and memory bank addressing**.
> - [ ] **Two-stage design is optimal** for N = 32, 64, 128, 256 using 8-to-1 sub-MUXs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demultiplexers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT416 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Demultiplexers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Demultiplexer (DEMUX / Data Distributor)** is a combinational logic circuit that takes a **single input data line** and routes it to one of **$2^n$ possible output lines**, where the selection of the output channel is controlled by an $n$-bit **select input (address)**. The output that is *not* selected is forced to a logic level (typically logic `0`, depending on whether the selected output is asserted as `1` or `0`).

Formally, a demultiplexer implements the Boolean function:

$$
Y_i = D \cdot m_i(S_{n-1}, S_{n-2}, \dots, S_0)
$$

where $D$ is the data input, $m_i$ is the $i^{th}$ **minterm** of the $n$ select lines, and $Y_i$ is the $i^{th}$ output line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> A demultiplexer performs the **reverse operation of a multiplexer**. It receives information from a single line and directs it to one of $2^n$ possible output lines, where the specific output is selected by the $n$ select inputs. It is therefore also called a **Data Distributor**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Signal Switchyard

Imagine a **single railway track entering a major railway station** from the countryside. As the single track enters, it passes through a large mechanical **switchyard (demultiplexer)**. A **signal controller at the switchyard** uses a set of levers (the **select lines**) to mechanically reroute the incoming single train onto **one of $2^n$ outgoing tracks** (platforms).

- The single incoming track → **Data Input $D$**
- The lever combination set by the controller → **Select Lines $S_0, S_1, \dots, S_{n-1}$**
- The $2^n$ outgoing tracks → **Output lines $Y_0, Y_1, \dots, Y_{2^n-1}$**
- The track that the train actually rolls onto → the **selected output (asserted to the same logic level as $D$)**
- All other tracks remain empty → **non-selected outputs stay at logic `0`**

This routing-by-selection mechanism is the *exact* functional behavior of a demultiplexer in digital hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Multiplexer:**
> - **MUX** = Many-to-One (selects one of $2^n$ inputs → 1 output)
> - **DEMUX** = One-to-Many (routes 1 input → one of $2^n$ outputs)

## 1.3 Standard Notation & Pin Description

For an **$n$-to-$2^n$** demultiplexer:

| Pin Category | Symbol | Count | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Input | $D$ | **1** | Serial data to be routed |
| Select Inputs | $S_0, S_1, \dots, S_{n-1}$ | $n$ | Binary address of target output |
| Outputs | $Y_0, Y_1, \dots, Y_{2^n-1}$ | $2^n$ | Destination channels |
| Enable (optional) | $\overline{E}$ | **0 or 1** | Active-low enable; disables all outputs when asserted |

The output lines are typically **active-HIGH**, meaning the selected output follows $D$ (replicates $D$) and non-selected outputs remain at logic `0`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

A 1-to-$2^n$ demultiplexer works as follows:

1. **Step 1 — Address Decoding:** The $n$ select lines $(S_{n-1}, \dots, S_0)$ form a binary address. The demux internally decodes this address into $2^n$ unique **minterms** $m_0, m_1, \dots, m_{2^n-1}$.
2. **Step 2 — AND-Gating with Data:** Each minterm is then AND-ed with the single data input $D$. The output line whose minterm matches the current select address will receive $D \cdot 1 = D$.
3. **Step 3 — Output Assertion:** All other output lines receive $D \cdot 0 = 0$.
4. **Step 4 (Optional) — Enable Control:** If an active-low enable $\overline{E}$ is present, the entire device is enabled only when $\overline{E} = 0$. When $\overline{E} = 1$, *all* outputs are forced to `0` regardless of $D$ or select lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Design:** AND-gating ensures that only the line whose minterm equals the current select code receives the data. Since minterms are mutually exclusive, exactly one output is ever active at a time.

## 2.2 The 1-to-2 Demultiplexer (Simplest Case)

For $n=1$ select line:

$$
Y_0 = D \cdot \overline{S}, \qquad Y_1 = D \cdot S
$$

### Truth Table

| $S$ | $Y_0$ | $Y_1$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | $D$ | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | $D$ |

## 2.3 The 1-to-4 Demultiplexer ($n = 2$)

For select lines $S_1 S_0$:

$$
Y_0 = D \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0}, \quad Y_1 = D \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0
$$
$$
Y_2 = D \cdot S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0}, \quad Y_3 = D \cdot S_1 \cdot S_0
$$

### Truth Table (1-to-4 DEMUX)

| $S_1$ | $S_0$ | $Y_0$ | $Y_1$ | $Y_2$ | $Y_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $D$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | $D$ | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $D$ | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $D$ |

## 2.4 The 1-to-8 Demultiplexer ($n = 3$)

For select lines $S_2 S_1 S_0$:

$$
Y_i = D \cdot m_i(S_2, S_1, S_0) \quad \text{for } i = 0 \text{ to } 7
$$

where the minterms expand to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_0 &= D \cdot \overline{S_2} \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \\
Y_1 &= D \cdot \overline{S_2} \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \\
Y_2 &= D \cdot \overline{S_2} \cdot S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \\
Y_3 &= D \cdot \overline{S_2} \cdot S_1 \cdot S_0 \\
Y_4 &= D \cdot S_2 \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0} \\
Y_5 &= D \cdot S_2 \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0 \\
Y_6 &= D \cdot S_2 \cdot S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0} \\
Y_7 &= D \cdot S_2 \cdot S_1 \cdot S_0
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of outputs | $N_{out} = 2^n$ | $n$ = number of select lines |
| 2 | Output equation (general) | $Y_i = D \cdot m_i$ | $m_i$ = minterm of select lines |
| 3 | Number of AND gates needed | $2^n$ | One per output |
| 4 | AND gate inputs per gate | $n + 1$ | $n$ from select + 1 from $D$ |
| 5 | Active-HIGH output rule | Selected $Y_i = D$; others $= 0$ | Boolean logic |
| 6 | Active-LOW output rule | Selected $Y_i = \overline{D}$; others $= 1$ | Inverted data |
| 7 | Enable function (active-LOW) | $Y_i = D \cdot m_i \cdot \overline{E}$ | $E$ = enable input |
| 8 | Boolean function generation | Set $D = 1$; $Y_i = m_i$ | Realizes any minterm |
| 9 | Sum-of-minterms identity | $F = \sum m_i = \text{OR}(Y_i)$ for selected $i$ | Direct from demux outputs |
| 10 | Demux tree expansion | Cascade 1-to-2 DEMUXes | $2^k$ levels for $n = 2^k$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Demultiplexers are Used in Industry:**

- **Memory Address Decoding:** A demux decodes a CPU's address bus to activate exactly one memory chip among many.
- **Data Routing in Communication Systems:** Serial data on a single line is fanned out to multiple receivers (TDM systems, UART channels).
- **Waveform / Function Generators:** A demux routes a clock or counter signal to one of several waveform-generation blocks.
- **ALU Output Distribution:** Routing computed results to specific registers based on a destination code.
- **LED / 7-Segment Display Driving:** Multiplexed display controllers use demux logic to drive row/column lines.
- **Boolean Function Implementation:** Any SOP expression can be realized by OR-ing the appropriate minterm outputs of a single demux (with $D=1$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Implementing a 3-Variable Boolean Function Using a 1-to-8 DEMUX

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Implement the Boolean function
$$F(A, B, C) = \sum m(1, 3, 5, 7)$$
using a **1-to-8 demultiplexer**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Demux Configuration
We have 3 variables, so we need a **1-to-8 DEMUX** with select lines $S_2 = A$, $S_1 = B$, $S_0 = C$. Tie the **data input $D = 1$** (logic HIGH).

### Step 2 — Set Up the Output Equations of the Demux
With $D = 1$, the demux outputs reduce to pure minterms:
$$Y_i = 1 \cdot m_i = m_i$$

So:
$$Y_0 = \overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,\overline{C}, \quad Y_1 = \overline{A}\,\overline{B}\,C, \quad Y_2 = \overline{A}\,B\,\overline{C}, \quad Y_3 = \overline{A}\,B\,C$$
$$Y_4 = A\,\overline{B}\,\overline{C}, \quad Y_5 = A\,\overline{B}\,C, \quad Y_6 = A\,B\,\overline{C}, \quad Y_7 = A\,B\,C$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Required Minterms
The function $F$ is the sum of minterms $1, 3, 5, 7$. So we need the demux outputs $Y_1, Y_3, Y_5, Y_7$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Identity
By definition of sum-of-minterms:
$$F = m_1 + m_3 + m_5 + m_7$$

Since $Y_i = m_i$ when $D = 1$:
$$F = Y_1 + Y_3 + Y_5 + Y_7$$

### Step 5 — Realize the Function
Connect a **single 4-input OR gate** to the demux outputs $Y_1, Y_3, Y_5, Y_7$ as shown in Section 4. The OR gate output is the desired function $F$.

> [!NOTE]
> **General KTU Insight:** Any $n$-variable Boolean function in SOP form can be implemented using a **single 1-to-$2^n$ DEMUX** plus **one OR gate**, by setting $D = 1$ and OR-ing the outputs corresponding to the minterms in the function.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Designing a 1-to-16 Demultiplexer Using Two 1-to-8 Demultiplexers (Demux Tree)

**Problem:** Construct a **1-to-16 DEMUX** using **two 1-to-8 DEMUXes** and **one 1-to-2 DEMUX** (or inverter). This is a classic KTU question.

### Step 1 — Count Required Devices
A 1-to-16 DEMUX requires $2^4 = 16$ outputs. Two 1-to-8 DEMUXes provide $8 + 8 = 16$ outputs.
A 1-to-2 DEMUX is used to route the data input $D$ to one of the two 1-to-8 DEMUXes based on the **MSB of the select code**.

### Step 2 — Partition the Select Lines
- $S_3$ (MSB) → controls the 1-to-2 DEMUX
- $S_2, S_1, S_0$ → common select lines to both 1-to-8 DEMUXes

### Step 3 — Analyze Routing Behavior

**Case 1:** $S_3 = 0$
- The 1-to-2 DEMUX routes $D$ to the **upper 1-to-8 DEMUX** (call it DEMUX-A).
- Within DEMUX-A, the select code $S_2 S_1 S_0$ activates one of $Y_0$ to $Y_7$.
- So $Y_i = D$ for $i = 0$ to $7$ when $S_3 = 0$.

**Case 2:** $S_3 = 1$
- The 1-to-2 DEMUX routes $D$ to the **lower 1-to-8 DEMUX** (call it DEMUX-B).
- Within DEMUX-B, outputs $Y_8$ to $Y_{15}$ are activated based on $S_2 S_1 S_0$.

### Step 4 — Formal Output Equations
For $i = 0, 1, \dots, 7$:
$$Y_i = D \cdot \overline{S_3} \cdot m_i(S_2, S_1, S_0)$$
For $i = 8, 9, \dots, 15$ (let $j = i - 8$):
$$Y_i = D \cdot S_3 \cdot m_j(S_2, S_1, S_0)$$

### Step 5 — Verification
- 4 select lines $\Rightarrow 2^4 = 16$ output combinations. ✓
- Two 8-output demuxes cover all 16 outputs. ✓
- Only one output is active at any time. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Demux Tree Rule:** To build a 1-to-$2^n$ DEMUX from smaller 1-to-$2^k$ DEMUXes, you need $\lceil n/k \rceil$ levels of cascading. The MSBs go to the highest-level demux (used for chip-enable), and the LSBs go to the leaf demuxes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Active-HIGH vs Active-LOW Output Demultiplexers

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU Board Exam — Active-LOW variant is frequently tested**

| Feature | Active-HIGH Output DEMUX | Active-LOW Output DEMUX |
|---|---|---|
| Selected output state | $Y_{sel} = D$ | $Y_{sel} = \overline{D}$ |
| Non-selected outputs | $Y_{others} = 0$ | $Y_{others} = 1$ |
| Boolean expression | $Y_i = D \cdot m_i$ | $Y_i = \overline{D \cdot m_i}$ |
| $D = 1$ behavior | $Y_{sel} = 1$, others $= 0$ | $Y_{sel} = 0$, others $= 1$ |
| Boolean function realization | $F = \sum Y_i$ (OR gate needed) | $F = \overline{\text{NAND}(Y_i)}$ (NAND gate) |
| Common IC example | 74LS138 (3-to-8, active-LOW out) | 74LS138 (functionally active-LOW) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: 4-Variable Function Implementation

**Problem:** Implement $F(A, B, C, D) = \sum m(2, 5, 9, 12, 15)$ using a 1-to-16 DEMUX.

### Step 1 — Demux Configuration
Use a 1-to-16 DEMUX with select lines $S_3 = A$, $S_2 = B$, $S_1 = C$, $S_0 = D$, and $D_{in} = 1$.

### Step 2 — Identify Outputs
With $D_{in} = 1$, each output $Y_i = m_i(A,B,C,D)$.

The required minterms are $\{2, 5, 9, 12, 15\}$.

### Step 3 — OR the Outputs
$$F = Y_2 + Y_5 + Y_9 + Y_{12} + Y_{15}$$

Connect a **5-input OR gate** to these five demux outputs to obtain $F$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students sometimes forget to set $D_{in} = 1$ for an active-HIGH output demux. If $D_{in} = 0$, *all* outputs are 0, and the function is always 0. Always explicitly state $D_{in} = 1$ in your answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic 1-to-$2^n$ Demultiplexer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN["INPUT SECTION"]
        D["Data Input D"]
        S0["S0"]
        S1["S1"]
        S2["Sn-1"]
    end
    subgraph CORE["DECODER + AND GATES CORE"]
        DEC["Binary Address Decoder"]
        A0["AND Gate"]
        A1["AND Gate"]
        A2["AND Gate"]
        An["AND Gate 2 to n"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT SECTION"]
        Y0["Y0"]
        Y1["Y1"]
        Y2["Y2"]
        Yn["Y2n-1"]
    end
    D --> A0
    D --> A1
    D --> A2
    D --> An
    S0 --> DEC
    S1 --> DEC
    S2 --> DEC
    DEC --> A0
    DEC --> A1
    DEC --> A2
    DEC --> An
    A0 --> Y0
    A1 --> Y1
    A2 --> Y2
    An --> Yn
```

## 4.2 Logic Gate Implementation: 1-to-4 Demultiplexer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D["Data D"]:::in
    S0["S0"]:::in
    S1["S1"]:::in
    N0["NOT S0"]:::g
    N1["NOT S1"]:::g
    A0["AND 3-input"]:::a
    A1["AND 3-input"]:::a
    A2["AND 3-input"]:::a
    A3["AND 3-input"]:::a
    Y0["Y0"]:::out
    Y1["Y1"]:::out
    Y2["Y2"]:::out
    Y3["Y3"]:::out
    S0 --> N0
    S1 --> N1
    N1 --> A0
    N1 --> A1
    S1 --> A2
    S1 --> A3
    N0 --> A0
    S0 --> A1
    N0 --> A2
    S0 --> A3
    D --> A0
    D --> A1
    D --> A2
    D --> A3
    A0 --> Y0
    A1 --> Y1
    A2 --> Y2
    A3 --> Y3
    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef g fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef a fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Boolean Function Realization via DEMUX

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEMUX["1-to-8 DEMUX with D=1"]
        Dfix["D = 1"]:::fixed
        SA["S2 = A"]:::in
        SB["S1 = B"]:::in
        SC["S0 = C"]:::in
        Y0["Y0 m0"]:::out
        Y1["Y1 m1"]:::hl
        Y2["Y2 m2"]:::out
        Y3["Y3 m3"]:::hl
        Y4["Y4 m4"]:::out
        Y5["Y5 m5"]:::hl
        Y6["Y6 m6"]:::out
        Y7["Y7 m7"]:::hl
    end
    OR["4-input OR gate"]:::or
    F["F A B C"]:::final
    Dfix --> Y0
    Dfix --> Y1
    Dfix --> Y2
    Dfix --> Y3
    Dfix --> Y4
    Dfix --> Y5
    Dfix --> Y6
    Dfix --> Y7
    SA --> DEMUX
    SB --> DEMUX
    SC --> DEMUX
    Y1 --> OR
    Y3 --> OR
    Y5 --> OR
    Y7 --> OR
    OR --> F
    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef fixed fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef hl fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef or fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The highlighted outputs $Y_1, Y_3, Y_5, Y_7$ are the minterms selected to implement the function $F(A,B,C) = \sum m(1, 3, 5, 7)$ as derived in Section 3.1. The single OR gate consolidates them.

## 4.4 Architecture: 1-to-16 Demux Tree (Cascading)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D["Data D"]:::in
    S0["S0"]:::in
    S1["S1"]:::in
    S2["S2"]:::in
    S3["S3 MSB"]:::in
    M2["1-to-2 DEMUX"]:::m
    M2A["1-to-8 DEMUX A Upper"]:::m
    M2B["1-to-8 DEMUX B Lower"]:::m
    Y0["Y0"]:::out
    Y7["Y7"]:::out
    Y8["Y8"]:::out
    Y15["Y15"]:::out
    D --> M2
    S3 --> M2
    M2 -->|S3=0| M2A
    M2 -->|S3=1| M2B
    S0 --> M2A
    S1 --> M2A
    S2 --> M2A
    S0 --> M2B
    S1 --> M2B
    S2 --> M2B
    M2A --> Y0
    M2A --> Y7
    M2B --> Y8
    M2B --> Y15
    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef m fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Tree:** The MSB $S_3$ steers $D$ into the upper demux (outputs $Y_0$–$Y_7$) or the lower demux (outputs $Y_8$–$Y_{15}$). The LSBs $S_2 S_1 S_0$ then pick the exact output within the selected 8-output bank.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a demultiplexer. How does it differ from a multiplexer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **demultiplexer (DEMUX)** is a combinational circuit that receives a **single input data line** and routes it to **one of $2^n$ output lines**, selected by $n$ select inputs. It functions as a **data distributor** and is essentially the reverse of a multiplexer.

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | Multiplexer | Demultiplexer |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Many-to-One | One-to-Many |
| Number of inputs | $2^n$ data inputs | 1 data input |
| Number of outputs | 1 | $2^n$ |
| Select lines | $n$ | $n$ |
| Operation | Selects one of many inputs to forward | Routes one input to one of many outputs |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for MUX vs DEMUX distinction, 1 mark for the comparison table)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three applications of demultiplexers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Memory address decoding** in microprocessors — the demux activates the exact memory chip corresponding to the address on the address bus.
2. **Serial-to-parallel data conversion** — a serial bit stream on a single line is distributed sequentially to parallel output channels.
3. **Implementation of Boolean functions** — any SOP Boolean function can be realized using a single demux and one OR gate by tying $D=1$.
4. *(Bonus point)*: **Time-division multiplexing systems** and **ALU output routing** in digital systems.

*(1 mark per application — any three accepted)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Two alternative question stems are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Design and explain the operation of a **1-to-4 demultiplexer**. Draw the truth table and logic diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement the Boolean function $F(A, B, C) = \sum m(0, 2, 4, 6)$ using a **1-to-8 demultiplexer**. Draw the logic diagram and explain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A 1-to-4 DEMUX has 1 data input $D$, 2 select inputs $S_1, S_0$, and 4 outputs $Y_0, Y_1, Y_2, Y_3$.

**Boolean Equations:**
$$Y_0 = D \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot \overline{S_0}, \quad Y_1 = D \cdot \overline{S_1} \cdot S_0$$
$$Y_2 = D \cdot S_1 \cdot \overline{S_0}, \quad Y_3 = D \cdot S_1 \cdot S_0$$

**Truth Table:**

| $S_1$ | $S_0$ | $Y_0$ | $Y_1$ | $Y_2$ | $Y_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $D$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | $D$ | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $D$ | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $D$ |

**Logic Circuit:** Requires **two NOT gates** (for $\overline{S_0}$ and $\overline{S_1}$) and **four 3-input AND gates** — one per output. Refer to the gate-level diagram in Section 4.2.

**Operation:** The select lines form a 2-bit address. The decoder inside the demux identifies which minterm is active. The AND gate corresponding to that minterm passes the data input $D$ to its output. All other AND gates receive a `0` minterm, forcing their outputs to `0`.

*(3 marks for Boolean equations + truth table, 2 marks for logic diagram, 2 marks for operational explanation)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Choose a 1-to-8 DEMUX with select inputs $S_2 = A$, $S_1 = B$, $S_0 = C$, and tie the data input to logic `1` (i.e., $D = V_{CC}$).

**Step 2:** With $D = 1$, each output $Y_i$ equals the $i^{th}$ minterm $m_i(A, B, C)$.

**Step 3:** The function is $F = \sum m(0, 2, 4, 6)$. Hence:
$$F = m_0 + m_2 + m_4 + m_6 = Y_0 + Y_2 + Y_4 + Y_6$$

**Step 4:** Connect a **4-input OR gate** to the demux outputs $Y_0, Y_2, Y_4, Y_6$. The output of this OR gate is the desired function $F$.

**Logic Diagram:**

```
   A ---- S2 ┐
   B ---- S1 ├──> [1-to-8 DEMUX, D=1] ── Y0 ─┐
   C ---- S0 ┘                           ── Y2 ─┤
                                            ── Y4 ─┼──> [OR] ──> F
                                            ── Y6 ─┘
```

**Verification:** Substituting each minterm, only $Y_0, Y_2, Y_4, Y_6$ can be `1` (and only one of the four at a time, but OR-ing all gives the SOP). All other outputs are `0`. ✓

*(2 marks for choosing D=1 and identifying outputs, 2 marks for the OR-gate connection, 2 marks for the final equation and verification, 1 mark for the diagram)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the design of a **1-to-8 demultiplexer** using **two 1-to-4 demultiplexers** and additional logic. Derive the output equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **1-to-16 demultiplexer** using **two 1-to-8 demultiplexers** and a **1-to-2 demultiplexer**. Show all connections clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Design Strategy:** A 1-to-8 DEMUX needs **3 select lines**. We split these as $S_2$ (MSB) for the chip-enable stage and $S_1, S_0$ as common select lines to both 1-to-4 DEMUXes.

**Required Components:**
- Two 1-to-4 DEMUX chips (call them DEMUX-A and DEMUX-B)
- One NOT gate (to invert $S_2$ for enabling DEMUX-A)
- $D$ connected to both demuxes as data input
- Outputs $Y_0$–$Y_3$ come from DEMUX-A; $Y_4$–$Y_7$ come from DEMUX-B

**Enable Logic:**
- DEMUX-A enable: $\overline{E_A} = \overline{S_2}$ (active only when $S_2 = 0$)
- DEMUX-B enable: $\overline{E_B} = S_2$ (active only when $S_2 = 1$)

**Output Equations:**
$$Y_i = D \cdot \overline{S_2} \cdot m_i(S_1, S_0) \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, 2, 3$$
$$Y_j = D \cdot S_2 \cdot m_{j-4}(S_1, S_0) \quad \text{for } j = 4, 5, 6, 7$$

**Block Diagram:**

```
                   ┌── S1 ──┐
   D ──┬──────────►│        │── Y0
       │           │ DEMUX-A│── Y1
   S2 ─┤── NOT ──E_A         │── Y2
       │           └─────────┘── Y3
       │
       └──────────E_B ┌─────────┐
                       │ DEMUX-B│── Y4
   S1 ─────────────────┤        │── Y5
   S0 ─────────────────┤        │── Y6
                       └─────────┘── Y7
```

*(3 marks for design strategy + enable logic, 2 marks for output equations, 2 marks for block diagram)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Design:** A 1-to-16 DEMUX needs **4 select lines** $S_3, S_2, S_1, S_0$ and **16 outputs** $Y_0$–$Y_{15}$.

**Partitioning:**
- Use **MSB $S_3$** to drive a **1-to-2 DEMUX** (data-routing stage)
- $S_2, S_1, S_0$ go to the **select lines of both 1-to-8 DEMUXes**

**Connections:**

1. **Data Input:** Connect $D$ to the data input of the **1-to-2 DEMUX** (the master router).
2. **1-to-2 DEMUX select:** Connect $S_3$ to the select input of the 1-to-2 DEMUX.
3. **1-to-2 DEMUX outputs:**
   - When $S_3 = 0$, the 1-to-2 DEMUX routes $D$ to **DEMUX-A** (upper 1-to-8)
   - When $S_3 = 1$, it routes $D$ to **DEMUX-B** (lower 1-to-8)
4. **Common Select Lines:** Connect $S_2, S_1, S_0$ to the select pins of both 1-to-8 DEMUXes in parallel.
5. **Output Mapping:**
   - DEMUX-A produces $Y_0$ to $Y_7$
   - DEMUX-B produces $Y_8$ to $Y_{15}$

**Output Equations (formal):**
$$Y_i = D \cdot \overline{S_3} \cdot m_i(S_2, S_1, S_0) \quad \text{for } i = 0, \dots, 7$$
$$Y_{i+8} = D \cdot S_3 \cdot m_i(S_2, S_1, S_0) \quad \text{for } i = 0, \dots, 7$$

**Total Hardware:**
- 1 × 1-to-2 DEMUX
- 2 × 1-to-8 DEMUX
- No additional gates required

*(3 marks for partitioning + routing, 2 marks for output mapping, 2 marks for hardware summary and justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to set $D = 1$** when implementing Boolean functions with a DEMUX — results in $F = 0$ always. *(-2 marks)*
> 2. **Mixing up MUX and DEMUX definitions** in Part A — *(-1 to -2 marks)*
> 3. **Incorrect enable-pin polarity** in cascade designs — students often connect the enable incorrectly and produce overlapping output assertions. *(-2 marks)*
> 4. **Not labeling all select lines and outputs** in diagrams — the examiner expects complete and unambiguous diagrams. *(-1 mark)*
> 5. **Active-HIGH vs Active-LOW confusion** when working with ICs like the 74LS138 — this IC has **active-LOW outputs**, and not accounting for this will give wrong Boolean function equations. *(-2 marks)*
> 6. **Failing to show the final OR-gate equation** in Boolean function implementation questions — must explicitly write $F = Y_a + Y_b + \dots$. *(-1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Demultiplexers**

- **Definition:** A DEMUX is a **1-to-$2^n$ data distributor** — one input routed to one of $2^n$ outputs based on an $n$-bit select code.
- **Output Equation:** $Y_i = D \cdot m_i(\text{select lines})$ — the data input is AND-ed with the $i^{th}$ minterm of the select lines.
- **Active-HIGH vs Active-LOW Outputs:**
  - Active-HIGH: Selected output = $D$, others = 0.
  - Active-LOW (e.g., 74LS138): Selected output = $\overline{D}$, others = 1.
- **Boolean Function Realization:**
  - Set $D = 1$.
  - $Y_i$ becomes minterm $m_i$.
  - Use a single OR gate to sum the required minterms.
- **Cascading (Demux Tree):**
  - To build a **1-to-$2^n$** DEMUX from smaller 1-to-$2^k$ DEMUXes, use $\lceil n/k \rceil$ levels.
  - The **MSBs** of the select code act as the **chip-enable** for the upper-level demux.
  - The **LSBs** of the select code go to the **leaf demuxes**.
- **Hardware Cost:**
  - A 1-to-$2^n$ DEMUX needs $2^n$ AND gates, each with $(n+1)$ inputs.
  - Plus $n$ NOT gates for select-line inversion.
- **Key Differences from Decoder:** A decoder activates an output based on the select code alone; a demux activates an output **based on the select code AND the data input**. Setting $D = 1$ makes a demux behave like a decoder.
- **Applications (Memorize):** Memory address decoding, serial-to-parallel conversion, Boolean function implementation, TDM systems, ALU output routing.
- **Standard IC Reference:** **74LS138** — a 3-to-8 decoder/demultiplexer with **active-LOW outputs** and **three enable pins** (two active-LOW, one active-HIGH).
- **KTU-Favorite Question Patterns:**
  - "Implement $F = \sum m(\dots)$ using a 1-to-$2^n$ DEMUX." *(3 to 7 marks)*
  - "Design a 1-to-16 DEMUX using smaller DEMUXes." *(7 marks)*
  - "Compare MUX and DEMUX." *(3 marks)*
  - "Explain the use of a DEMUX as a Boolean function generator." *(5–7 marks)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shifters - Barrel Shifter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT416 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shifters - Barrel Shifter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1 — Shifters: Barrel Shifter

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Barrel Shifter** is a **combinational digital circuit** capable of shifting or rotating an *N*-bit data word by any number of bit positions (from **0 to N−1**) in a **single clock cycle** (or more precisely, in **one propagation delay**). It is implemented as an array of multiplexers and is a critical building block in the datapath of processors, ALUs, and DSP units.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rotary Telephone Dial" 🚲

Imagine a **circular platform with 8 seats** (representing 8 bits). People can be arranged in any rotational position. Now imagine an **instructor** who can spin this platform by **0, 1, 2, ..., or 7 seats** by simply pressing a single button.

- **Normal shift register** → People must walk out one-by-one (takes *k* clock cycles for *k* shifts).
- **Barrel shifter** → The entire platform *rotates as a unit* through a hardwired multiplexer network in **ONE operation**.

That is the magic of a barrel shifter — **parallel, single-cycle, configurable shifting**.

### 1.1.2 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlights)

| Metric | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Shift Range | $0$ to $N-1$ bits | For an $N$-bit barrel shifter |
| Number of Control Lines | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | To select shift amount |
| Number of MUX Levels | $\log_2 N$ | In a hierarchical design |
| Single-Cycle Operation | Yes | Combinational, not sequential |
| Data Direction | Bidirectional | Left, Right, Rotate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Barrel shifters are classified as **combinational MSI (Medium Scale Integration)** circuits and are explicitly listed under *Data Path Design* in Module 1 of PEBMT416.

---

## 1.2 Types of Shifting Operations

A barrel shifter must support the following fundamental operations:

1. **Logical Shift Left (LSL)** — Bits shifted out from MSB are lost; LSB is filled with **0**.
2. **Logical Shift Right (LSR)** — Bits shifted out from LSB are lost; MSB is filled with **0**.
3. **Circular Shift / Rotate Left (ROL)** — MSB wraps around to become the new LSB.
4. **Circular Shift / Rotate Right (ROR)** — LSB wraps around to become the new MSB.
5. **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASR)** — MSB is **replicated** (sign-extension for signed numbers).

---

## 1.3 Why Barrel Shifters? — Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a conventional **serial shift register**, shifting a 32-bit word by 16 positions consumes **16 clock cycles**. In a pipelined RISC processor, this creates a massive **pipeline stall / structural hazard**.

The **barrel shifter** eliminates this bottleneck entirely by performing the shift in a **single combinational propagation delay**, making it indispensable in:
- **ALU datapaths** of CPUs (e.g., ARM, x86 shift instructions).
- **Floating-point units** for exponent alignment during addition/subtraction.
- **DSP processors** for FFT butterfly operations.
- **Cryptographic hardware** for bit permutation in block ciphers (DES, AES).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Philosophy

A barrel shifter is essentially an **N × N switching matrix**, where each output bit can be sourced from **any one of the N input bits**, selected by a *log₂(N)*-bit control word.

For an **N-bit barrel shifter** with shift control $S = s_{k-1} s_{k-2} \dots s_1 s_0$ (where $k = \log_2 N$):

$$Y_i = X_{(i + S) \bmod N}$$

where $Y_i$ is the *i*-th output bit, $X_j$ is the *j*-th input bit, and $S$ is the unsigned shift amount.

## 2.2 Two Principal Design Approaches

### Approach 1 — Direct (Crossbar) Implementation
- Uses **N × N** 2-to-1 MUXes.
- Each output bit has its own dedicated column of N MUXes.
- Hardware count: **N² 2×1 MUXes**.

### Approach 2 — Hierarchical (Multi-Stage) Implementation ⭐ *(KTU Favourite)*
- Uses **log₂(N)** stages of MUXes.
- Stage *j* handles bit *sⱼ* of the shift control.
- Hardware count: **N × log₂(N) 2×1 MUXes**.
- Drastically reduces transistor count for large N.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU board exam question on barrel shifters.**

| Parameter | Formula | Example (N=8) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of control bits (k) | $k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $k = 3$ |
| Maximum shift amount | $N - 1$ | $7$ |
| Direct MUX count | $N^2$ | $64$ |
| Hierarchical MUX count | $N \cdot \log_2 N$ | $24$ |
| Number of MUX stages | $\log_2 N$ | $3$ |
| Propagation delay (stages × MUX delay) | $t_{pd} \cdot \log_2 N$ | $3 \cdot t_{pd}$ |
| Hardware saving ratio | $1 - \dfrac{\log_2 N}{N}$ | $62.5\%$ saved |

> **Notation Note:** $\vert x \vert$ denotes absolute value; $\bmod$ denotes the modulo operator; both are written without using the markdown pipe character to preserve table formatting.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Barrel Shifter |
|---|---|
| **RISC Processors (ARM, MIPS)** | Implements the *LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR* instructions in single cycle |
| **Floating-Point Units** | Aligns mantissas before addition |
| **DSP / FFT Engines** | Performs bit-reversal addressing and butterfly rotations |
| **Memory Management Units** | Implements barrel shift for address translation |
| **Cryptography (DES/AES)** | Performs fast bit permutations |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Design of a 4-Bit Barrel Shifter (Hierarchical Method)

This is the **most frequently asked KTU question**. We will design it rigorously.

### Given
- Data input: $X = x_3 x_2 x_1 x_0$ (4-bit word)
- Shift control: $S = s_1 s_0$ (2 bits → can encode 0, 1, 2, 3 shifts)
- Output: $Y = y_3 y_2 y_1 y_0$

### Step 1 — Determine Control Encoding

| $s_1$ | $s_0$ | Shift Amount ($S$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | 2 |
| 1 | 1 | 3 |

### Step 2 — Determine Output Mapping (Right Rotation by $S$ positions)

For each output bit $y_i$, we select the input bit:

$$y_i = x_{(i + S) \bmod 4}$$

### Step 3 — Build the Truth Table for Each Output Bit

**Output $y_0$ (LSB):**

| $s_1$ | $s_0$ | $y_0$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $x_0$ |
| 0 | 1 | $x_1$ |
| 1 | 0 | $x_2$ |
| 1 | 1 | $x_3$ |

**Output $y_1$:**

| $s_1$ | $s_0$ | $y_1$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $x_1$ |
| 0 | 1 | $x_2$ |
| 1 | 0 | $x_3$ |
| 1 | 1 | $x_0$ |

**Output $y_2$:**

| $s_1$ | $s_0$ | $y_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $x_2$ |
| 0 | 1 | $x_3$ |
| 1 | 0 | $x_0$ |
| 1 | 1 | $x_1$ |

**Output $y_3$ (MSB):**

| $s_1$ | $s_0$ | $y_3$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | $x_3$ |
| 0 | 1 | $x_0$ |
| 1 | 0 | $x_1$ |
| 1 | 1 | $x_2$ |

### Step 4 — Multi-Stage MUX Realization (2 Levels)

**Stage 1** uses control line $s_0$ (handles shifts of 0 or 1):

$$y_0^{(1)} = \overline{s_0}\, x_0 + s_0\, x_1$$
$$y_1^{(1)} = \overline{s_0}\, x_1 + s_0\, x_2$$
$$y_2^{(1)} = \overline{s_0}\, x_2 + s_0\, x_3$$
$$y_3^{(1)} = \overline{s_0}\, x_3 + s_0\, x_0$$

**Stage 2** uses control line $s_1$ (handles shifts of 0 or 2, applied on top of Stage 1):

$$y_0 = \overline{s_1}\, y_0^{(1)} + s_1\, y_2^{(1)} = \overline{s_1}\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_0 + s_0\, x_1) + s_1\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_2 + s_0\, x_3)$$

$$y_1 = \overline{s_1}\, y_1^{(1)} + s_1\, y_3^{(1)} = \overline{s_1}\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_1 + s_0\, x_2) + s_1\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_3 + s_0\, x_0)$$

$$y_2 = \overline{s_1}\, y_2^{(1)} + s_1\, y_0^{(1)} = \overline{s_1}\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_2 + s_0\, x_3) + s_1\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_0 + s_0\, x_1)$$

$$y_3 = \overline{s_1}\, y_3^{(1)} + s_1\, y_1^{(1)} = \overline{s_1}\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_3 + s_0\, x_0) + s_1\,(\overline{s_0}\, x_1 + s_0\, x_2)$$

### Step 5 — Hardware Count

$$\text{Total MUXes} = N \times \log_2 N = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ 2×1 MUXes}$$

$$\text{Direct-method comparison} = N^2 = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ 2×1 MUXes}$$

$$\text{Hardware saved} = 1 - \frac{N \log_2 N}{N^2} = 1 - \frac{\log_2 N}{N} = 1 - \frac{2}{4} = 50\%$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Serial Shift Register | Direct Barrel Shifter | Hierarchical Barrel Shifter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of clock cycles for max shift | $N - 1$ | **1** | **1** |
| Hardware (gates) | $N$ flip-flops | $N^2$ MUXes | $N \log_2 N$ MUXes |
| Critical path delay | $1$ flip-flop delay | $N \cdot t_{MUX}$ | $\log_2 N \cdot t_{MUX}$ |
| Power consumption | Lower (sequential) | Higher (combinational) | Medium |
| Typical use case | Serial communication | Small-width custom logic | **Processor datapaths** ⭐ |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Design an 8-bit barrel shifter that supports right rotation by 0 to 7 positions. Calculate:
> (a) The number of control lines.
> (b) The number of 2×1 MUXes required using the hierarchical approach.
> (c) The number of MUX stages and the propagation delay in terms of $t_{pd}$.

### Solution

**(a) Number of control lines:**
$$k = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3 \text{ lines } (s_2, s_1, s_0)$$

**(b) Number of 2×1 MUXes (hierarchical):**
$$\text{MUXes} = N \cdot \log_2 N = 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ MUXes}$$

**(c) Number of stages and propagation delay:**
$$\text{Stages} = \log_2 N = 3$$
$$t_{\text{critical}} = 3 \cdot t_{pd}$$

**Comparison with direct method:**
$$\text{Direct MUXes} = N^2 = 64$$
$$\text{Savings} = 1 - \frac{24}{64} = 62.5\%$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 4-Bit Hierarchical Barrel Shifter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT VECTORS"]
        x0["X0 INPUT BIT 0"]
        x1["X1 INPUT BIT 1"]
        x2["X2 INPUT BIT 2"]
        x3["X3 INPUT BIT 3"]
    end

    subgraph S0["STAGE 1 MUXES CONTROLLED BY S0 SHIFT BY 0 OR 1"]
        m00["MUX00 SELECTS X0 OR X1"]
        m11["MUX11 SELECTS X1 OR X2"]
        m22["MUX22 SELECTS X2 OR X3"]
        m33["MUX33 SELECTS X3 OR X0"]
    end

    subgraph S1["STAGE 2 MUXES CONTROLLED BY S1 SHIFT BY 0 OR 2"]
        n0["MUXF0 SELECTS MUX00 OR MUX22"]
        n1["MUXF1 SELECTS MUX11 OR MUX33"]
        n2["MUXF2 SELECTS MUX22 OR MUX00"]
        n3["MUXF3 SELECTS MUX33 OR MUX11"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT VECTORS"]
        y0["Y0 OUTPUT BIT 0"]
        y1["Y1 OUTPUT BIT 1"]
        y2["Y2 OUTPUT BIT 2"]
        y3["Y3 OUTPUT BIT 3"]
    end

    x0 --> m00
    x1 --> m00
    x1 --> m11
    x2 --> m11
    x2 --> m22
    x3 --> m22
    x3 --> m33
    x0 --> m33

    m00 --> n0
    m22 --> n0
    m11 --> n1
    m33 --> n1
    m22 --> n2
    m00 --> n2
    m33 --> n3
    m11 --> n3

    n0 --> y0
    n1 --> y1
    n2 --> y2
    n3 --> y3

    ctrlS0["CTRL S0"] -.-> m00
    ctrlS0 -.-> m11
    ctrlS0 -.-> m22
    ctrlS0 -.-> m33

    ctrlS1["CTRL S1"] -.-> n0
    ctrlS1 -.-> n1
    ctrlS1 -.-> n2
    ctrlS1 -.-> n3
```

## 4.2 Control Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SHIFT CONTROL S s1 s0 2 BITS"] --> B["STAGE 1 CONTROLLER s0 ROUTES EITHER INPUT OR INPUT PLUS 1"]
    B --> C["STAGE 2 CONTROLLER s1 ROUTES EITHER STAGE1 OR STAGE1 PLUS 2"]
    C --> D["FINAL OUTPUT Y y3 y2 y1 y0 4 BITS"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Truth Table — Right Rotation by $S$ (4-bit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    table["TRUTH TABLE FOR 4 BIT BARREL SHIFTER RIGHT ROTATION"]
    row1["S EQUALS 0 ZERO SHIFT Y EQUALS X3 X2 X1 X0"]
    row2["S EQUALS 1 SHIFT BY 1 Y EQUALS X0 X3 X2 X1"]
    row3["S EQUALS 2 SHIFT BY 2 Y EQUALS X1 X0 X3 X2"]
    row4["S EQUALS 3 SHIFT BY 3 Y EQUALS X2 X1 X0 X3"]

    table --> row1
    table --> row2
    table --> row3
    table --> row4
```

## 4.4 MUX Array Layout for an 8-Bit Hierarchical Barrel Shifter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S8["8 BIT BARREL SHIFTER TOP VIEW"]
        I0["IN0"] --> L1A["STAGE1 S0 MUX"]
        I1["IN1"] --> L1A

        I2["IN2"] --> L1B
        I2 --> L1C["STAGE1 S0 MUX"]
        I3["IN3"] --> L1C
        I3 --> L1D["STAGE1 S0 MUX"]
        I4["IN4"] --> L1D
```
