> **Design rule (KTU 2024):** Set the PLL natural frequency $\omega_n$ such that the *jitter peaking* (typically $1.4$ for $\zeta = 0.707$) does *not* amplify the CDR's incoming jitter at the data-rate sideband. A common rule: $f_n \le f_{bit}/1667$ for SONET.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU ESE Style

**Problem:** A 2.5 Gbps serial link has a clock with $f_0 = 2.5\,\text{GHz}$ and phase noise $\mathcal{L}(f) = -90\,dBc/Hz$ at $f = 1\,\text{MHz}$, $-110\,dBc/Hz$ at $f = 10\,\text{MHz}$, and $-130\,dBc/Hz$ at $f = 100\,\text{MHz}$. The system exhibits $DJ_{p-p} = 30\,ps$. The target $BER = 10^{-12}$ and $\sigma_{RJ} = 2\,ps$. Find the **total jitter** and the **eye opening** in UI.

**Solution — Step A: Compute $n$.**
$n = 14.069$ for $BER = 10^{-12}$.

**Solution — Step B: Total jitter (RJ contribution).**
$n \cdot \sigma_{RJ} = 14.069 \times 2\,\text{ps} = 28.138\,\text{ps}$.

**Solution — Step C: Total jitter (combined).**
$TJ(10^{-12}) = DJ_{p-p} + n \cdot \sigma_{RJ} = 30\,\text{ps} + 28.138\,\text{ps} = 58.138\,\text{ps}$.

**Solution — Step D: Unit Interval.**
$UI = 1/f_{bit} = 1/(2.5 \times 10^9) = 400\,\text{ps}$.

**Solution — Step E: Eye opening in UI.**
$\text{Eye opening} = 1 - TJ/UI = 1 - 58.138/400 = 1 - 0.1453 = 0.8547\,UI$.

> [!TIP]
> The eye is open to $0.8547\,UI$ at $10^{-12}$ — a healthy link. If the eye opening fell below $0.3\,UI$, a **repeater, equalizer, or FEC** would be required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Jitter Generation, Transfer, and Tolerance

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Ideal Clock or Data Source] --> GEN[Functional Block with JGEN]
    GEN --> CHAN[Channel - PCB Trace or Cable - adds ISI]
    CHAN --> XTLK[Crosstalk Aggressors - adds BUJ]
    XTLK --> RX[Receiver CDR and Equalizer]
    RX --> JTOL[Jitter Tolerance Mask - SRC compliance check]
    GEN --> JGENM[JGEN Measurement at output of functional block]
    RX --> JTRM[Jitter Transfer Function H of s]
    SRC --> JIN[Input Jitter JIN]
    JIN --> RX
    RX --> JOUT[Output Jitter JOUT at sampling instant]
    JOUT --> EYE[Eye Diagram and Q Factor]
    EYE --> BER[BER Estimation]

    A1[Random Jitter RJ from thermal and shot noise] --> GEN
    A2[Periodic Jitter PJ from supply noise] --> GEN
    A3[Duty Cycle Distortion DCD] --> GEN
    A4[Inter Symbol Interference ISI] --> CHAN
    A5[Bounded Uncorrelated Jitter BUJ] --> XTLK

    BER --> DEC{Decision}
    DEC -->|BER OK| PASS[Pass - link compliant]
    DEC -->|BER fails| FAIL[Fail - reduce jitter or add repeater]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Mermaid — Decomposition of Total Jitter at the Sampling Eye

```mermaid
graph LR
    IDEAL[Ideal Edge - zero jitter] --> RJ[Random Component - Gaussian tail]
    IDEAL --> DJ[Deterministic Component - bounded peaks]
    RJ --> TAIL[Bell Shaped Probability Density Function]
    DJ --> PEAK[Dirac Like Probability Density Function]
    TAIL --> CONV[Convolution of RJ and DJ pdfs]
    PEAK --> CONV
    CONV --> TJEYE[Total Jitter Histogram at BER 10 power minus 12]
    TJEYE --> UI[Unit Interval Window - 1 UI]
    TJEYE --> TJBUDGET[Jitter Budget TJ = DJpp + n times sigma RJ]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Follow the data flow left-to-right. The *first* diagram maps a complete SERDES system: generation → propagation → reception → compliance. The *second* diagram isolates the *statistical* process that gives rise to the bathtub curve and the $TJ(BER)$ formula.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define jitter. Distinguish clearly between **Random Jitter (RJ)** and **Deterministic Jitter (DJ)** with respect to their physical origin, statistical distribution, and boundedness. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Jitter is the short-term deviation of a digital signal's transition edges from their ideal timing positions.
**Random Jitter (RJ):** originates from intrinsic device noise (thermal, shot, flicker); follows an *unbounded Gaussian distribution*; cannot be eliminated, only minimized by design.
**Deterministic Jitter (DJ):** originates from *systematic* effects (ISI, PJ, DCD, BUJ, cross-talk); follows a *bounded* probability distribution; can in principle be removed by good design, equalization, or deskew.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain **period jitter** and **cycle-to-cycle jitter**. Why is cycle-to-cycle jitter the *most stringent* specification for a clock multiplier? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
**Period Jitter ($J_{PER}$):** the deviation of any single clock period $T_n$ from the *mean* period $\bar{T}$ over the observation window: $J_{PER} = T_n - \bar{T}$.
**Cycle-to-Cycle Jitter ($J_{CC}$):** the change in period from one cycle to the next: $J_{CC} = T_{n+1} - T_n$.
**Why most stringent:** $J_{CC}$ measures the *acceleration* of timing error between two adjacent cycles. A clock multiplier (e.g., PLL) might have a slowly drifting mean (long-term jitter large) but tight $J_{CC}$, and this is the parameter that determines whether downstream flip-flops will see back-to-back setup violations. It is therefore the *high-frequency* content of jitter and the hardest to filter.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Derive the expression* $TJ(BER) = DJ_{p-p} + n \cdot \sigma_{RJ}$ for total jitter at a given bit error rate, clearly stating the role of the **dual-Dirac model** and the value of $n$ for $BER = 10^{-12}$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 5 Gbps SERDES link has an **oscillator** with phase noise $-100\,dBc/Hz$ at $1\,MHz$ offset, $-120\,dBc/Hz$ at $10\,MHz$, and a flat floor at $-140\,dBc/Hz$ from $100\,MHz$ to $500\,MHz$. Compute the **RMS jitter** $\sigma_{jitter}$ and the **total jitter at $BER = 10^{-12}$** assuming $DJ_{p-p} = 25\,ps$. Express the answer in **ps** and in **UI**, and comment on link margin. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — BER from a single Gaussian tail.**
A single bit sampled in the presence of Gaussian timing noise has error probability:

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{W}{\sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{RJ}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Dual-Dirac assumption.**
Deterministic jitter produces two delta-like peaks separated by $DJ_{p-p}$. Each peak has its own Gaussian tail of width $\sigma_{RJ}$. The total BER is the sum of the two tail probabilities, which for a symmetric eye gives:

$$BER = \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{W}{\sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{RJ}}\right) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,n}e^{-n^2/2} \quad\text{(asymptotic)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $n$ at $BER = 10^{-12}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \sqrt{2}\;\text{erfc}^{-1}(2 \times 10^{-12}) \\
  &= 14.069 \quad \text{(standard telecom sigma count)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compose the total jitter.**
The total eye closure at the sampling instant is the deterministic span $DJ_{p-p}$ plus $n$ sigmas of Gaussian noise:

$$TJ(BER) = DJ_{p-p} + n \cdot \sigma_{RJ}$$

**Step 5 — State physical meaning.**
$TJ$ is the *eye opening reduction* (peak-to-peak) required to achieve a given BER. The equation reveals that *only* the RJ component scales with $n$ — DJ is BER-independent.

---

#### **Model Answer A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Convert $\mathcal{L}(f)$ to linear.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 = 1\,\text{MHz} &: \quad 10^{-100/10} = 10^{-10} \\
f_2 = 10\,\text{MHz} &: \quad 10^{-120/10} = 10^{-12} \\
f_3 = 500\,\text{MHz} &: \quad 10^{-140/10} = 10^{-14}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Approximate the phase-noise integral in three bands.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= 10^{-10} \times (10 - 1)\times10^{6} = 0.9 \times 10^{-3} = 9 \times 10^{-4} \\
I_2 &= 10^{-12} \times (100 - 10)\times10^{6} = 9 \times 10^{-5} \\
I_3 &= 10^{-14} \times (500 - 100)\times10^{6} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \\
I_{total} &= 9.0 \times 10^{-4} + 9.0 \times 10^{-5} + 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \\
         &= 9.94 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the phase-noise-to-jitter formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{jitter} &= \frac{1}{2\pi f_0}\sqrt{2\,I_{total}} \\
f_0 &= 5\,\text{GHz} = 5\times10^{9}\,\text{Hz} \\
2\pi f_0 &= 3.142 \times 10^{10}\,\text{rad/s} \\
\sqrt{2 \times 9.94 \times 10^{-4}} &= \sqrt{1.988 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04459 \\
\sigma_{jitter} &= \frac{0.04459}{3.142 \times 10^{10}} = 1.419 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{s} \\
\sigma_{jitter} &= 1.42\,\text{ps (RMS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total jitter at $BER = 10^{-12}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TJ &= DJ_{p-p} + n \cdot \sigma_{RJ} \\
   &= 25\,\text{ps} + 14.069 \times 1.42\,\text{ps} \\
   &= 25 + 19.98 = 44.98\,\text{ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to UI.** $UI = 1/5\,\text{GHz} = 200\,\text{ps}$.

$$TJ_{UI} = \frac{44.98}{200} = 0.2249\,UI$$

**Step 6 — Eye opening and link margin.**

$$\text{Eye opening} = 1 - 0.2249 = 0.7751\,UI \approx 0.78\,UI$$

**Comment:** The eye is open to **78% of the unit interval** — an excellent link margin. The dominant contributor is *deterministic* jitter (25 ps / 45 ps ≈ 56%), so equalization rather than oscillator redesign would yield the biggest improvement.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the four principal *components* of deterministic jitter — **Periodic Jitter (PJ)**, **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**, **Duty Cycle Distortion (DCD)**, and **Bounded Uncorrelated Jitter (BUJ)** — with a one-line physical origin and a typical magnitude. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A clock-recovery PLL has $K_{PD} = 1\,\text{mA/rad}$, $K_{VCO} = 1\,\text{GHz/V}$, charge-pump current $I_{CP} = 1\,\text{mA}$, loop filter $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 100\,\text{pF}$, and divider $N = 10$. The input data rate is $f_{bit} = 1\,\text{Gbps}$. Compute the **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and **damping factor** $\zeta$ of the second-order PLL, and use the jitter transfer function to determine the *jitter peaking* in dB. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer B(a) — 7 marks**

| Component | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Periodic Jitter (PJ)** | Sinusoidal-like timing modulation from **power-supply noise**, switching harmonics, or coupling from a nearby clock | 1–50 ps |
| **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** | **Bandwidth-limited channel** (PCB trace, connector, cable) — high-frequency content of NRZ pulses is attenuated, smearing edges | 5–100 ps |
| **Duty Cycle Distortion (DCD)** | **Mismatch in rise vs. fall times** of clock buffers, threshold asymmetry, or device $V_{TH}$ mismatch | 1–20 ps |
| **Bounded Uncorrelated Jitter (BUJ)** | **Cross-talk** from neighbouring aggressors that are *uncorrelated* with the victim data | 1–30 ps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DCD is *not* the same as PJ. DCD has its effect concentrated on *one* edge of the clock (e.g., every rising edge is early while every falling edge is on time), whereas PJ affects *both* edges equally and is periodic in time.

---

#### **Model Answer B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify PLL parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{PD} &= 1\,\text{mA/rad} = 10^{-3}\,\text{A/rad} \\
K_{VCO} &= 1\,\text{GHz/V} = 2\pi \times 10^{9}\,\text{rad/(s·V)} \\
I_{CP} &= 1\,\text{mA} = 10^{-3}\,\text{A} \\
R &= 10^4\,\Omega,\quad C = 10^{-10}\,\text{F} \\
N &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Loop filter time constants.**

$$
\tau_1 = RC = 10^4 \times 10^{-10} = 10^{-6}\,\text{s}
$$

For a Type-II second-order PLL, $\tau_2$ is taken from the second integrator; with the given single-pole filter we use the standard form:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO}}{N \cdot \tau_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{10^{-3} \times 2\pi \times 10^9}{10 \times 10^{-6}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{PD} \cdot K_{VCO} &= 10^{-3} \times 2\pi \times 10^{9} = 2\pi \times 10^{6}\,\text{s}^{-2} \\
\frac{K_{PD}K_{VCO}}{N \tau_1} &= \frac{2\pi \times 10^{6}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 2\pi \times 10^{11}\,\text{s}^{-2} \\
\omega_n &= \sqrt{2\pi \times 10^{11}} = 7.92 \times 10^{5}\,\text{rad/s} \\
f_n &= \omega_n / (2\pi) = 1.26 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Hz} \approx 126\,\text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Damping factor.** For a standard passive lead-lag filter with second pole from the charge-pump output impedance, $\tau_2 \approx C \cdot R_2$ where $R_2$ is small; assuming a modest second pole, $\zeta \approx 0.707$ (Butterworth design). Then:

$$\zeta = 0.707 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{peaking at } f_n = 0\,\text{dB (Butterworth)}$$

**Step 5 — Jitter peaking for general $\zeta$.**

$$
\text{Peaking} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)\;\text{dB (for } \zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}\text{)}
$$

For $\zeta = 0.707$ the denominator equals $1$, so peaking $= 0\,dB$. If $\zeta = 0.5$, peaking $= 20\log_{10}(1/\sqrt{3}) = -4.77\,dB$ (actually attenuation); for $\zeta = 0.4$, peaking $= +2.5\,dB$.

**Step 6 — Sanity check vs. data rate.**

$$f_n / f_{bit} = 126\,\text{kHz} / 1\,\text{GHz} = 1.26 \times 10^{-4}$$

This satisfies the **SONET guideline** $f_n \le f_{bit}/1667 \approx 600\,\text{kHz}$ — the PLL is well within the compliant region.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$** inside $\text{erfc}^{-1}$ when solving for $n$ — costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Mixing up period jitter vs. cycle-to-cycle jitter** definitions — examiners *will* deduct if $J_{CC}$ is described as deviation from the mean.
> 3. **Using $\mathcal{L}(f)$ in $dBc/Hz$ directly inside an integral** — you must convert to linear ($10^{\mathcal{L}/10}$) first. A 2-mark deduction is typical.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert $TJ$ to UI** when asked for eye opening — show both.
> 5. **Confusing jitter transfer peaking with jitter generation** — peaking is a *transfer* property (input vs. output), generation is an *added* property. State which one your formula addresses.
> 6. **Writing $TJ = DJ + RJ$** (without $n$) — this loses 1 mark because it ignores the BER dependence of the Gaussian tail.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Jitter** is a *time-domain* metric; **phase noise** is its *frequency-domain* counterpart. They are Fourier transform pairs.
- **Total Jitter = DJ + n·σ_RJ**, where $n = 14.069$ for $BER = 10^{-12}$ and $n = 15.94$ for $BER = 10^{-15}$.
- **RJ** is *unbounded* and *Gaussian*; **DJ** is *bounded* and *deterministic*.
- The **dual-Dirac model** is the KTU-board standard for combining RJ and DJ.
- **Period jitter** = $T_n - \bar{T}$; **cycle-to-cycle jitter** = $T_{n+1} - T_n$.
- **Phase noise to jitter formula:** $\sigma_{jitter} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_0}\sqrt{2\int 10^{\mathcal{L}(f)/10}\,df}$.
- **Jitter budget:** $TJ_{budget} = UI - t_{setup} - t_{hold} - t_{tx} - t_{rx}$ — every system design must satisfy this inequality.
- **PLL jitter transfer** is a **2nd-order low-pass** with damping $\zeta$; peaking must be controlled per standard (SONET, PCIe, SATA, USB3).
- **Jitter has three engineering faces:** *generation* (what the block adds), *transfer* (how the block filters), *tolerance* (what the block can absorb). All three appear in compliance masks.
- **Eye opening** $= 1 - TJ/UI$. A healthy link needs eye opening $\ge 0.3\,UI$ at the target BER; $< 0.3\,UI$ requires **FEC, equalization, or repeaters**.
- The four **DJ sub-types** to remember: **PJ, ISI, DCD, BUJ** — every KTU DJ question maps to one of these.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "J-I-T-T-E-R"**
> **J**itter = deviation in time. **I**ntegrate phase noise → jitter RMS. **T**otal jitter = DJ + n·RJ. **T**wo types: bounded DJ + unbounded RJ. **E**ye opening falls by TJ. **R**emember $n = 14$ for $10^{-12}$ — exam day lifesaver.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gating the Clock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT416 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gating the Clock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Clock Gating** is a sequential design technique used to **selectively enable or disable the clock signal** that drives one or more flip-flops (or registers) in a synchronous digital system. By masking the clock pulses reaching the storage elements during periods of inactivity, clock gating **eliminates unnecessary switching activity** in the clocked elements, thereby **reducing dynamic power dissipation** and suppressing unwanted data transitions.

The technique operates by **ANDing** (positive-level gating) or **ORing** (negative-level gating) the global clock with a *gate-enable* control signal, producing a *gated clock* output that is distributed only to the sequential cells whose state must change.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Global Clock ($CLK$)** | The primary periodic timing reference distributed across the chip. |
| **Gate-Enable Signal ($EN$)** | Synchronous control input deciding whether the clock is propagated or blocked. |
| **Gated Clock ($CLK_{gated}$)** | The output of the gating network fed to the clock pin of the target flip-flops. |
| **Active Edge** | The rising (or falling) edge of $CLK$ that the flip-flop uses to capture data. |
| **Glitch** | An unwanted transient pulse on $CLK_{gated}$ caused by the *enable* signal changing while the clock is high (or low). |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of clock gating like the electric switch in your hostel room.** The *electricity mains* in the building is constantly running at $50\ \text{Hz}$ (this is your **global clock $CLK$**). The *switch on the wall* is your **gate-enable signal $EN$**. The *bulb inside the room* only flickers when you close the switch — meaning the bulb sees a *gated* power waveform ($CLK_{gated}$). When the switch is open, even though the mains is alive, the bulb does not waste energy converting electricity into heat/light. Similarly, when $EN = 0$, the flip-flops downstream do not toggle and hence do not consume dynamic switching power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The analogy carries a **critical engineering warning**: just as a *chattering* (bouncing) wall switch can cause the bulb to flicker unpredictably, a **poorly synchronized $EN$** signal can produce **glitches** on $CLK_{gated}$ that falsely trigger the flip-flops. The hardware design must therefore *guarantee* that $EN$ transitions only when the clock is in its inactive phase.

---

## 1.3 Physical/Design Constants & Metrics

The following **bold** parameters are central to any KTU numerical on this topic:

- **Duty Cycle (D)**: The fraction of one clock period during which the clock is logically HIGH.
  $$D = \frac{t_{HIGH}}{T_{clk}} \quad \text{(ideal = 0.5)}$$
- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd,gating}$)**: Delay introduced by the gating logic. Typical CMOS value: **50 ps – 500 ps**.
- **Hold Time ($t_{hold}$)**: Minimum time data must remain stable after the active clock edge. For a 90 nm cell it is approximately **30 ps – 80 ps**.
- **Setup Time ($t_{setup}$)**: Typically **40 % of $T_{clk}$** in modern FPGAs.
- **Skew ($t_{skew}$)**: Maximum allowable difference between clock edges reaching two adjacent registers. For a 100 MHz clock the safe skew is **$\leq 0.5\ \text{ns}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard KTU board convention is to take **$V_{DD} = 1.8\ \text{V}$** for $0.18\ \mu\text{m}$ CMOS and **$V_{DD} = 1.2\ \text{V}$** for $90\ \text{nm}$ CMOS unless explicitly stated otherwise. Dynamic power is proportional to $V_{DD}^{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why We Gate the Clock — The *Why* and *How*

### The Power-Consumption Justification
The dynamic power dissipated by a CMOS gate per clock cycle is:
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$$
where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$)
- $C_{L}$ = load capacitance at the output node
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency

In a **synchronous register** that holds an unchanged value across $N$ idle cycles, $\alpha = 0$, so the *data* output does not dissipate dynamic power. **However**, the *clock pin* of that flip-flop continues to toggle on every active edge, charging and discharging the internal clock-tree capacitance $C_{clk}$ with $\alpha = 1$. This is the **"useless toggling"** that clock gating eliminates.

> When $EN = 0$, the gated clock is held LOW (or HIGH), so the internal capacitance of the disabled flip-flops is **not** charged/discharged, and the term $\alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot f_{clk}$ effectively drops to zero for that part of the circuit.

### Operational Logic — The *How*

**Step 1 — Sample the enable synchronously.**
The enable $EN$ must be generated by a flip-flop that is clocked by the *same* $CLK$. This ensures $EN$ changes only on active clock edges.

**Step 2 — Combine with the clock via a logic gate.**
A 2-input AND, OR, NAND, or NOR gate is used depending on whether the active edge is rising or falling.

**Step 3 — Distribute the gated clock.**
The $CLK_{gated}$ signal is routed (preferably through a clock buffer tree) to the clock pins of the target registers.

**Step 4 — Observe timing constraints.**
- $EN$ must be **stable** for at least $t_{setup} + t_{hold}$ around the active edge of $CLK$ to prevent metastability.
- The gating gate must add at most $t_{clk} - t_{setup}$ delay to keep the active edge within the target's setup window.

---

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Gating Topologies

### 2.2.1 Positive-Level (AND) Gating — used for rising-edge flip-flops

$$CLK_{gated} = CLK \cdot EN$$

| $EN$ | $CLK$ | $CLK_{gated}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 (clock suppressed) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 (clock passes) |

- Active edge: **rising edge of $CLK$**.
- When $EN = 0$, the gated clock is forced LOW, so no rising edge reaches the flip-flop.

### 2.2.2 Negative-Level (OR) Gating — used for falling-edge flip-flops

$$CLK_{gated} = CLK + \overline{EN}$$

| $EN$ | $CLK$ | $CLK_{gated}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | 1 (clock suppressed) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 (clock passes during 1) |

- Active edge: **falling edge of $CLK$**.
- When $EN = 0$, the gated clock is held HIGH, so no falling edge reaches the flip-flop.

> [!NOTE]
> The $OR$ form is mathematically expressed as $CLK_{gated} = CLK + \overline{EN}$ so that a *de-asserted* enable forces the output HIGH regardless of $CLK$. This is the conventional textbook way of writing it; some authors equivalently write $CLK_{gated} = \overline{\overline{CLK} \cdot EN}$ using a NOR/NAND construction.

---

## 2.3 The Glitch Problem — *Why Naïve Gating Fails*

If $EN$ is **asynchronous** (i.e., changes while $CLK = 1$ for AND-gating, or while $CLK = 0$ for OR-gating), the output of the gating gate may produce a **spurious pulse** shorter than one full clock period. This is a **clock glitch**.

**Numerical condition for a glitch (AND-gate case):**

$$\text{Glitch exists} \iff \big(EN \text{ transitions while } CLK = 1\big)$$

Mathematically, the AND output can be written:
$$CLK_{gated}(t) = CLK(t) \cdot EN(t)$$

If $EN$ changes from $0 \to 1$ at instant $t_0$ with $CLK(t_0) = 1$, then a **partial HIGH pulse** of width $(T_{clk}/2 - \delta t)$ appears, where $\delta t$ is the delay of the enable relative to the active edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Such a glitch has enough energy to be interpreted by the flip-flop as a *legitimate active edge*, causing **erroneous data latching** and **functional failure** of the design.

---

## 2.4 Glitch-Free (Latch-Based) Clock Gating

The standard KTU board-mandated solution is the **latch-and-gate** structure:

1. Place a **negative-level-sensitive D-latch** whose data input is $EN$ and whose enable is $CLK$.
2. Feed the latch output $\overline{EN}_{latched}$ into the AND/OR gating gate.

**Operation:**
- When $CLK = 0$ (clock LOW), the latch is **transparent**, so $EN$ flows to the gating gate and may safely change.
- When $CLK = 1$ (clock HIGH), the latch is **opaque**, holding the previously-sampled value of $EN$. The input to the AND gate cannot change, hence **no glitch** is possible.

$$CLK_{gated} = CLK \cdot \big(\overline{EN}_{\text{held when }CLK=1}\big)$$

The latch is therefore called the **glitch-suppressing element**.

---

## 2.5 ⚙ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power per gate | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $D = t_{HIGH} / T_{clk}$ | Duty cycle of clock | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_{clk} = 1 / f_{clk}$ | Clock period | seconds (s) |
| 4 | $E_{toggle} = C_{L} V_{DD}^{2}$ | Energy per 0→1 transition | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $P_{saved} = \alpha_{old} C_{L} V_{DD}^{2} f_{clk}$ | Power saved by gating | W |
| 6 | $t_{w,glitch} \le t_{pd,gate} + t_{skew}$ | Maximum glitch width | s |
| 7 | $t_{hold,total} = t_{hold,FF} + t_{cd,latch}$ | Effective hold time | s |
| 8 | $CLK_{gated} = CLK \cdot EN$ | AND-gate model | logic level |
| 9 | $CLK_{gated} = CLK + \overline{EN}$ | OR-gate model | logic level |
| 10 | $f_{max} \le 1 / (t_{setup} + t_{ck2q} + t_{comb})$ | Maximum safe clock freq | Hz |

> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` in this table to keep it Markdown-safe (KTU examiner note).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series):** Over **80 %** of the clocked registers are gated during typical workloads, saving hundreds of milliwatts.
- **FPGA Design (Xilinx 7-series, Intel Cyclone):** Synthesis tools automatically insert *BUFGCE* (clock enable) primitives that are latch-based to provide glitch-free gating.
- **Microcontroller Sleep Modes:** The ARM Cortex-M0+ uses clock gating on the entire AHB bus to drop standby current to **< 1 µA**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Percentage Power Reduction by Clock Gating

Consider an $N$-bit register with clock load capacitance $C_{clk}$ per flip-flop, switching on every active edge as long as the register is *enabled*. Out of $T$ total clock cycles, the register is *active* for $K$ cycles and *idle* for $(T - K)$ cycles.

**Step 1 — Energy per transition at each clock pin.**
From basic CMOS physics, the energy dissipated by charging a capacitance $C$ through voltage $V_{DD}$ is $\frac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^{2}$, and an equal amount is dissipated on discharge. The total energy per full 0→1→0 swing is therefore:
$$E_{clk} = C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy without gating.**
Without gating, the register toggles on **every** clock edge, so the total number of toggles is $N \cdot T$ (one toggle per bit per cycle):
$$E_{no\_gate} = N \cdot T \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy with clock gating.**
With gating, the register toggles only during the $K$ active cycles:
$$E_{gate} = N \cdot K \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy saved.**
$$\Delta E = E_{no\_gate} - E_{gate} = N (T - K) \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction.**
$$\eta_{saved} = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{no\_gate}} = \frac{T - K}{T} = 1 - \frac{K}{T}$$

This shows that **the power saved equals exactly the fraction of time the register is idle**.

> **Numerical illustration for KTU:** For a 32-bit register, $V_{DD} = 1.8\ \text{V}$, $C_{clk} = 25\ \text{fF}$, $T = 1000$ cycles, $K = 200$ active cycles. Compute $\eta_{saved}$ and the absolute energy saved.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{saved} &= 1 - \frac{200}{1000} = 0.8 = 80\% \\[4pt]
\Delta E &= 32 \cdot (1000 - 200) \cdot 25 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.8)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 32 \cdot 800 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 3.24 \\[4pt]
&= 32 \cdot 800 \cdot 81 \times 10^{-15} \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}073{,}600 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{J} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0736\ \text{nJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The clock-gating technique saves **80 %** of the clock-tree dynamic energy, equivalent to **2.07 nJ** per 1000 clock cycles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Allowable Gating Delay

A flip-flop has setup time $t_{setup} = 100\ \text{ps}$ and the clock period is $T_{clk} = 4\ \text{ns}$ (corresponding to $f_{clk} = 250\ \text{MHz}$). The clock buffer that drives the gating gate has an intrinsic delay of $t_{buf} = 200\ \text{ps}$. The combinational path feeding the data input has $t_{ck2q} = 150\ \text{ps}$ and $t_{comb} = 1.2\ \text{ns}$.

**Step 1 — Total available timing budget.**
$$t_{budget} = T_{clk} - t_{setup} = 4\ \text{ns} - 0.1\ \text{ns} = 3.9\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the already-consumed path delay.**
$$t_{data\_path} = t_{ck2q} + t_{comb} = 0.15\ \text{ns} + 1.2\ \text{ns} = 1.35\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 3 — Compute remaining slack for the gating delay.**
$$t_{gate,max} = t_{budget} - t_{data\_path} - t_{buf} = 3.9 - 1.35 - 0.2 = 2.35\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to maximum number of 2-input NAND equivalents.**
A 2-input NAND has typical delay $t_{NAND} \approx 0.1\ \text{ns}$ in a 90 nm cell library.
$$N_{NAND,max} = \frac{t_{gate,max}}{t_{NAND}} = \frac{2.35}{0.1} \approx 23\ \text{NANDs}$$

> **Conclusion:** The gating network may consist of up to **23 cascaded 2-input NAND gates** (≈ 23 × 0.1 ns = 2.3 ns) before the design violates timing at 250 MHz.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Condition for Glitch Generation in AND-Gating

Let the enable signal $EN$ switch at instant $t_e$ with slope $t_r$ (rise time), and let the clock signal $CLK$ have period $T$ and be HIGH during interval $(0, T/2)$ for a 50 % duty cycle.

**Step 1 — Express $EN(t)$ during its transition.**

Assuming a linear ramp from 0 to 1 across $[t_e, t_e + t_r]$:
$$EN(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < t_e \\ \frac{t - t_e}{t_r}, & t_e \le t \le t_e + t_r \\ 1, & t > t_e + t_r \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Express $CLK_{gated}(t) = CLK(t) \cdot EN(t)$.**
If $t_e \in (0, T/2)$ (i.e., $CLK = 1$ during the transition), then for $t \in [t_e, t_e + t_r]$:
$$CLK_{gated}(t) = 1 \cdot \frac{t - t_e}{t_r}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the glitch.**
Because $EN$ was 0 just before $t_e$, the AND output was 0. The output then *ramps up* (in the discrete digital sense, rises) at $t_e + t_{pd,AND}$, stays HIGH for the remainder of the clock HIGH phase, and falls when $CLK$ falls. This is a **partial pulse** of width:
$$t_{w,glitch} = \frac{T}{2} - t_e + t_{pd,AND}$$

**Step 4 — Glitch condition.**
$$\boxed{\ \text{Glitch} \iff t_e \in \left(0,\ \frac{T}{2}\right)\ \text{ for AND-gating}\ }$$

**Step 5 — Glitch-free guarantee with negative-edge latch.**
The latch closes on the rising edge of $CLK$ at $t = 0$ and opens again on the falling edge at $t = T/2$. Hence $EN$ is **blocked** during $(0, T/2)$, eliminating the glitch.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Gating Style

| Design Priority | AND Gating | OR Gating | Latch + AND | Latch + OR |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Rising-edge FFs (D, JK) | ✅ Best | ❌ Wrong polarity | ✅✅ Glitch-free | ⚠ Redundant |
| Falling-edge FFs | ❌ Wrong polarity | ✅ Best | ⚠ Redundant | ✅✅ Glitch-free |
| Lowest hardware cost | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠ +1 latch | ⚠ +1 latch |
| Lowest power at idle | ❌ Clk pin still toggles via AND | ❌ | ✅ Truly stopped | ✅ Truly stopped |
| Suitable for asynchronous EN | ❌ Glitches | ❌ Glitches | ✅ Safe | ✅ Safe |

> **KTU 2024 board-recommended answer for any "design a clock-gating circuit" question:** use **latch + AND** for rising-edge flip-flops with synchronous enable.

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem (12-mark analogue):** A digital processor has $R = 4096$ flip-flops. Each flip-flop has an internal clock load capacitance $C_{clk} = 30\ \text{fF}$. The clock frequency is $f = 500\ \text{MHz}$ and $V_{DD} = 1.0\ \text{V}$. In a typical workload, each flip-flop is actively switching for only 30 % of the time. Compute:
1. The total clock-tree dynamic power **without** clock gating.
2. The total clock-tree dynamic power **with** clock gating.
3. The annual energy savings (in Joules) if the processor runs continuously for 1 year.

**Step 1 — Number of active cycles per second and inactive cycles per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{active} &= R \cdot f \cdot \rho = 4096 \cdot 500 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.30 \\[4pt]
&= 6.144 \times 10^{11}\ \text{toggles/sec} \\[4pt]
N_{idle} &= R \cdot f \cdot (1 - \rho) = 4096 \cdot 500 \times 10^{6} \cdot 0.70 \\[4pt]
&= 1.4336 \times 10^{12}\ \text{toggles/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Power without clock gating (all FFs toggle every cycle).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{no\_gate} &= R \cdot f \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 4096 \cdot 500 \times 10^{6} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.0)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 4096 \cdot 500 \times 10^{6} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-15} \\[4pt]
&= 61.44 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} = 61.44\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power with clock gating (only 30 % of FFs toggle).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gate} &= N_{active} \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 6.144 \times 10^{11} \cdot 30 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.0 \\[4pt]
&= 18.432 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W} = 18.43\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual energy saved.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 61.44 - 18.43 = 43.01\ \text{mW} \\[4pt]
T_{year} &= 365.25 \cdot 24 \cdot 3600 = 3.156 \times 10^{7}\ \text{s} \\[4pt]
\Delta E_{year} &= \Delta P \cdot T_{year} \\[4pt]
&= 43.01 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.156 \times 10^{7} \\[4pt]
&= 1.358 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.36\ \text{MJ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answers:**
- $P_{no\_gate} = 61.44\ \text{mW}$
- $P_{gate} = 18.43\ \text{mW}$
- $\Delta E_{year} \approx 1.36\ \text{MJ/year}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Clock-Gating Subsystem

The following Mermaid block diagram describes the *signal flow* of a latch-based, glitch-free clock-gating subsystem feeding an $N$-bit register file. This is the architecture every KTU board answer should be able to reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clock Source CLK 500 MHz] -->|fanout| B[Clock Buffer Tree]
    A -->|fanout| C[Enable Register D-FF]
    D[External Enable EN async] -->|data in| C
    C -->|EN sync| E[Negative Level D Latch]
    B -->|inverted enable| E
    E -->|EN latched held during CLK high| F[2 input AND Gate]
    B -->|CLK| F
    F -->|CLK gated| G[Clock Distribution Buffer]
    G -->|fanout 4096| H[N bit Register File]
    H -->|Q outputs| I[Combinational Logic Next Stage]
    I -->|feedback to D| J[Next State D inputs]
    J -->|data in| H
```

> The diagram shows that the **clock source $CLK$** fans out into three branches: (1) the clock buffer that drives the **AND gate**, (2) the inverted enable of the **latch** (so the latch is transparent while $CLK = 0$), and (3) the clock of the **enable synchronising register** that converts the external $EN$ into a synchronous version.

---

## 4.2 Finite State Machine — Glitch Generation in Naïve AND Gating

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IdleLow
    IdleLow --> WaitRising: CLK = 0, EN = 0
    WaitRising --> PropagatedHigh: CLK rises with EN = 1
    WaitRising --> GlitchDetected: EN changes 0 to 1 while CLK = 1
    PropagatedHigh --> PropagatedLow: CLK falls
    GlitchDetected --> ErroneousLatch: partial pulse shorter than T/2
    ErroneousLatch --> [*]
    PropagatedLow --> WaitRising: next cycle
```

> The state diagram encodes the analytical result from §3.3: if and only if $EN$ transitions during the clock-HIGH phase does the FSM enter the **GlitchDetected → ErroneousLatch** path. With a negative-edge latch in front of the AND gate, the FSM cannot leave the **PropagatedHigh** or **PropagatedLow** states through the glitch branch.

---

## 4.3 Timing Diagram (Text-Based Gantt Representation)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Clock Gating Timing Relationship
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Global Clock CLK
    CLK LOW phase 1         :a1, 0, 2
    CLK HIGH phase 1        :a2, 2, 2
    CLK LOW phase 2         :a3, 4, 2
    CLK HIGH phase 2        :a4, 6, 2
    section Enable Signal EN
    EN deasserted            :b1, 0, 3
    EN rising edge inside CLK high :crit, b2, 3, 1
    EN asserted              :b3, 4, 4
    section Naive AND Gate Output
    Glitch partial pulse    :crit, c1, 3, 1
    Stable 0 before glitch  :c2, 0, 3
    Stable 1 after glitch   :c3, 4, 4
    section Latch + AND Output
    No glitch clean 0      :d1, 0, 8
    Clean gated clock      :d2, 8, 4
```

> The Gantt chart visually contrasts the **naïve AND output** (which contains a `crit` (critical/red) partial pulse during the $EN$ transition inside $CLK$-HIGH) with the **latch + AND output** that remains cleanly at logic 0 throughout the same interval, proving the *glitch suppression* property of the latch.

---

## 4.4 Hierarchical Power-Saving Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Processor Core Active] --> Q{Is functional unit busy?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Enable = 1]
    Q -- No --> S[Enable = 0]
    R --> T[AND gate passes clock]
    S --> U[AND gate blocks clock]
    T --> V[Flip flops toggle as normal]
    U --> W[Flip flops frozen no switching]
    V --> X[Power consumption normal P_n]
    W --> Y[Power consumption near 0]
    X --> Z[Net power P_total = P_n + sum of P_idle]
    Y --> Z
    Z --> AA[Power saved by gating]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **forget to mention the latch** when describing glitch-free gating, losing 4 of 14 marks.
> - Marks are deducted if the answer uses asynchronous $EN$ feeding the AND gate directly without justification.
> - The duty cycle and edge polarity (rising vs falling) **must** be specified; never write "AND-gate" without stating it is for *positive-edge* flip-flops.
> - Always state the **timing constraint** ($t_{hold}$, $t_{setup}$) explicitly in derivations.

---

## 5.1 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define clock gating. State one advantage and one disadvantage of clock gating in synchronous digital circuits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Clock gating is a low-power design technique in which the clock signal driving a register or flip-flop is logically *AND-ed* (or *OR-ed*) with a synchronous enable signal, so that the clock pulses are propagated to the storage element only when its state is required to change. When the enable is de-asserted, the clock pin is held inactive, preventing redundant toggling and saving dynamic power.
- **Advantage (0.5 mark):** Significant reduction in dynamic switching power, proportional to the fraction of idle time.
- **Disadvantage (0.5 mark):** Naïve gating can introduce glitches on the gated clock, leading to erroneous data latching; hence a latch-based structure is required, which adds hardware cost and delay.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a positive-edge triggered D flip-flop with clock gating using an AND gate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The gated clock is generated as $CLK_{gated} = CLK \cdot EN$. The AND gate's truth table:

| $EN$ | $CLK$ | $CLK_{gated}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | X | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 (rising edge is passed) |

When $EN = 1$, every rising edge of $CLK$ produces a rising edge on $CLK_{gated}$ that latches $D$ into $Q$. When $EN = 0$, the AND output is held LOW irrespective of $CLK$, so the flip-flop sees no active edge and holds its previous state. (1 mark for diagram, 1.5 marks for explanation, 0.5 mark for stating the limitation about glitches.)

---

## 5.2 📕 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> The following are **TWO completely independent alternative questions** as per KTU ESE pattern. A student answers either **Question A** or **Question B**.

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Part (a) (7 marks):** *Explain with a block diagram how a latch-based clock-gating circuit eliminates glitches. Show the timing relationship between $CLK$, $EN$, latch output, and $CLK_{gated}$.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block diagram description (3 marks):**
The latch-based glitch-free clock gating circuit consists of:
- A **negative-level-sensitive D-latch** with data input $EN_{sync}$ and gate input $\overline{CLK}$ (or equivalently enable = $CLK$).
- A **2-input AND gate** whose inputs are $CLK$ and the latch's $Q$ output.
- The AND output is the **gated clock** $CLK_{gated}$ driving the target register file.

**2. Operating principle (2 marks):**
- When $CLK = 0$: the latch is *transparent*, so any change in $EN$ propagates to the AND gate input, but $CLK$ is also 0, so the AND output stays 0. **No glitch possible.**
- When $CLK = 1$: the latch is *opaque*, freezing the value of $EN$ that was sampled on the most recent rising edge. The AND input cannot change while $CLK = 1$, so the output is guaranteed to be a clean HIGH (if $EN$ was 1) or clean LOW (if $EN$ was 0) for the entire clock-HIGH phase. **No glitch possible.**

**3. Timing diagram (2 marks) — describe in text since the board allows any clear depiction:**

| Time (ns) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $CLK$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| $EN$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| Latch $Q$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $CLK_{gated}$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> The latch is transparent for $CLK=0$ (so $EN$ may change safely at $t=1$ and $t=5$) and opaque for $CLK=1$ (so the latch $Q$ is held constant during the high phase, guaranteeing a glitch-free $CLK_{gated}$).

---

**Part (b) (7 marks):** *A 16-bit register in a DSP processor operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2\ \text{V}$ and $f_{clk} = 200\ \text{MHz}$. The clock pin capacitance per flip-flop is $C_{clk} = 20\ \text{fF}$. If clock gating disables the register for 75 % of the cycles, calculate the power saved per second and the percentage reduction in dynamic power.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data.**
$R = 16$, $V_{DD} = 1.2\ \text{V}$, $f_{clk} = 200 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$, $C_{clk} = 20 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{F}$, $\rho = 0.25$ (active fraction), idle fraction = $0.75$.

**Step 2 — Power without clock gating.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{no\_gate} &= R \cdot f_{clk} \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 16 \cdot 200 \times 10^{6} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.2)^{2} \\[4pt]
&= 16 \cdot 200 \times 10^{6} \cdot 20 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 \\[4pt]
&= 16 \cdot 200 \cdot 20 \cdot 1.44 \times 10^{-9} \\[4pt]
&= 92.16 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W} \\[4pt]
&= 92.16\ \mu\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power with clock gating.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gate} &= \rho \cdot P_{no\_gate} = 0.25 \cdot 92.16 \\[4pt]
&= 23.04\ \mu\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power saved.**

$$
\Delta P = P_{no\_gate} - P_{gate} = 92.16 - 23.04 = 69.12\ \mu\text{W}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction.**

$$
\eta = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{no\_gate}} \times 100\% = \frac{69.12}{92.16} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Final answers:**
- Power saved = **69.12 µW**
- Percentage reduction = **75 %**

---

### ⭐ **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Part (a) (7 marks):** *Compare positive-level (AND) and negative-level (OR) clock gating. Under what circumstances would you prefer each? What is the role of the polarity of the active clock edge?*

**Model Solution:**

**1. AND gating — positive-level (3 marks):**
- Boolean expression: $CLK_{gated} = CLK \cdot EN$.
- Suitable for **positive-edge-triggered (rising-edge) flip-flops**.
- When $EN = 0$, $CLK_{gated}$ is held LOW, suppressing rising edges.
- **Limitation:** if $EN$ changes during $CLK = 1$, a glitch occurs.

**2. OR gating — negative-level (3 marks):**
- Boolean expression: $CLK_{gated} = CLK + \overline{EN}$.
- Suitable for **negative-edge-triggered (falling-edge) flip-flops**.
- When $EN = 0$, $\overline{EN} = 1$, so $CLK_{gated}$ is held HIGH, suppressing falling edges.
- **Limitation:** glitches arise if $EN$ changes during $CLK = 0$.

**3. Choice criteria (1 mark):**
- Choose AND gating when the design uses **rising-edge** registers (the most common in standard-cell libraries).
- Choose OR gating when the design uses **falling-edge** registers (rare; usually only in double-data-rate memories or specific high-speed paths).
- The active edge of the flip-flop must **match the polarity of the gate**; otherwise the flip-flop is permanently enabled or permanently disabled.

---

**Part (b) (7 marks):** *A 64-bit register is clocked at $f_{clk} = 1\ \text{GHz}$ with $V_{DD} = 0.9\ \text{V}$ and $C_{clk} = 15\ \text{fF}$. The enable signal is asynchronously asserted 200 ps after the rising edge of the clock, while the clock is still HIGH (clock HIGH window = 500 ps). Show with calculations whether a glitch will appear at the AND-gate output and, if so, compute its approximate width.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the glitch condition.**
For AND gating, a glitch appears if $EN$ changes while $CLK = 1$.

Given:
- $EN$ rises at $t_e = 200\ \text{ps}$ (relative to the rising edge of $CLK$ at $t=0$).
- Clock HIGH window = $500\ \text{ps}$ (i.e., $CLK$ falls at $t = 500\ \text{ps}$).

Since $t_e = 200\ \text{ps}$ lies in the interval $(0, 500\ \text{ps})$, the condition for a glitch is satisfied. **A glitch WILL appear.**

**Step 2 — Compute the glitch width.**
The AND gate propagates HIGH from $t_e + t_{pd,AND}$ to the falling edge of $CLK$. Assuming $t_{pd,AND} \approx 50\ \text{ps}$ for a 90 nm cell:

$$
t_{w,glitch} = t_{CLK,fall} - (t_e + t_{pd,AND}) = 500 - (200 + 50) = 250\ \text{ps}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to flip-flop minimum pulse-width tolerance.**
A typical 90 nm flip-flop requires a clock pulse width of at least $t_{pw,min} \approx 150\ \text{ps}$ to be recognised. Since $250\ \text{ps} > 150\ \text{ps}$, the glitch has sufficient energy to be falsely interpreted as a legitimate active edge, causing **erroneous data latching**.

**Step 4 — Remediation.**
Insert a negative-edge-sensitive D-latch between the $EN$ source and the AND gate. The latch closes on the rising edge of $CLK$, freezing the value of $EN$ throughout the HIGH phase and thereby eliminating the glitch entirely.

**Final answer:** Glitch width ≈ **250 ps**; the design as stated is **unsafe** and must be replaced with a latch + AND structure.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- ⏱ **Clock gating** = logical combination of $CLK$ with an enable to suppress redundant toggles → saves dynamic power $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$.
- ⏱ **Two flavours:** AND gating ($CLK_{gated} = CLK \cdot EN$) for rising-edge FFs; OR gating ($CLK_{gated} = CLK + \overline{EN}$) for falling-edge FFs.
- ⏱ **Glitch condition:** $EN$ changes while $CLK$ is in its *active* phase (HIGH for AND, LOW for OR).
- ⏱ **Glitch-free solution:** Insert a *negative-level-sensitive D-latch* in front of the AND/OR gate; latch is transparent when $CLK$ is in the *inactive* phase.
- ⏱ **Power saved fraction = idle fraction = $(1 - \rho)$**, where $\rho$ is the activity ratio.
- ⏱ **Timing constraint:** $t_{gate,max} \le T_{clk} - t_{setup} - t_{ck2q} - t_{comb} - t_{buf}$.
- ⏱ **Standard CMOS parameters (KTU convention):** $V_{DD} = 1.8\ \text{V}$ for $0.18\ \mu\text{m}$, $V_{DD} = 1.2\ \text{V}$ for $90\ \text{nm}$, $V_{DD} = 0.9\ \text{V}$ for $65\ \text{nm}$.
- ⏱ **For rising-edge FFs the canonical answer is "negative-edge D-latch + 2-input AND gate".**
- ⏱ **Engineering impact:** clock gating is the *single most effective* dynamic-power-reduction technique in modern CMOS SoCs; the industry reports savings of 30–70 % of total chip power.
- ⏱ **FPGA implementation:** equivalent primitives are `BUFGCE` (Xilinx) and `clkena` (Intel/Altera) — both internally use a latch.
- ⏱ **Examiner trap:** students often quote the formula $P = C V^{2} f$ but forget the activity factor $\alpha$; include it.
- ⏱ **Examiner trap:** always mention both *dynamic* and *short-circuit* power when justifying the design choice; gating reduces *both* because the clock pin is not transitioning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asynchronous Inputs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT416 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Asynchronous Inputs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Asynchronous Inputs — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Mano & Ciletti terminology):**
> *Asynchronous inputs* are flip-flop inputs that operate independently of the **clock (CLK)** signal. They directly change the output state of the flip-flop the moment they are asserted, **without waiting for a clock edge transition**. The two standard asynchronous inputs in any D, JK, or T flip-flop are **Preset (PRE)** and **Clear (CLR)** — also called *direct set* and *direct reset* (or *direct inputs*).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Design of Logic Systems (PEBMT416)*, asynchronous inputs are examined as a critical feature of edge-triggered and master-slave flip-flops. Unlike the synchronous data inputs (D, J, K, T) that are sampled only at the **active clock transition**, asynchronous inputs act with a propagation delay of only one or two gate delays — bypassing the clocked control logic entirely.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **school assembly** (the synchronous, clocked mode of operation) where students line up strictly when the bell rings. The bell is the **clock edge** — the synchronized trigger. However, the **fire alarm** is an asynchronous event: when it sounds, students must leave **immediately**, ignoring the bell. The fire alarm is your **asynchronous input** — it overrides the routine, clocked behavior and forces an immediate state change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering reality:** Asynchronous inputs are usually **active-LOW** (indicated by a bubble `◉` on the flip-flop symbol). When PRE = **0** and CLR = **1**, Q is forced to **1**; when CLR = **0** and PRE = **1**, Q is forced to **0**. The "do-nothing" condition is **PRE = CLR = 1** (both inactive).

## Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Propagation delay (asynchronous path):** typically **2 ns to 10 ns** in standard TTL/CMOS families (e.g., 74LS74, 74HC74, 74FCT374).
- **Recovery time (t_rec):** the minimum time **after the asynchronous input is deactivated** that the clock edge must wait for the flip-flop to respond predictably — typical values **5 ns to 25 ns**.
- **Removal time (t_rem):** the minimum time the asynchronous input must be removed **before the active clock edge** — typically **5 ns to 20 ns**.
- **Metastability mean time between failures (MTBF):** governed by the equation involving flip-flop resolution time, clock frequency, and asynchronous event rate (covered in Section 2).
- **Setup time (t_s) of synchronous inputs:** typically **3 ns to 20 ns** — must be satisfied regardless of asynchronous activity.
- **Hold time (t_h) of synchronous inputs:** typically **0 ns to 5 ns**.
- **Clock-to-Q delay (t_clk→Q):** typically **5 ns to 15 ns**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the **asynchronous truth table** of the JK/D flip-flop and ask students to draw a **timing diagram** showing clocked and asynchronous operations interleaved. Master both formats!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Asynchronous Inputs: Preset and Clear

In any edge-triggered flip-flop (D, JK, T, or SR), two special pins are designed to **bypass the clocked synchronous section**. These are the **asynchronous inputs**, and they form a dedicated *direct-control* path to the internal latch.

| Input | Symbol | Active Level | Effect on Q | Effect on Q' |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Preset | **PRE** (or $\overline{S_D}$ / $\overline{PS}$) | Active-LOW (0) | Forces Q → **1** | Q' → **0** |
| Clear | **CLR** (or $\overline{R_D}$ / $\overline{CR}$) | Active-LOW (0) | Forces Q → **0** | Q' → **1** |
| Both inactive | **PRE = 1, CLR = 1** | — | Flip-flop operates on **clocked synchronous inputs** | — |
| Both active | **PRE = 0, CLR = 0** | — | **Invalid / Unpredictable** state (Q = Q' = 1 or both race) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Asserting both PRE and CLR simultaneously is an **invalid condition** in most flip-flops. The internal SR latch becomes ambiguous, leading to a non-deterministic output when both are released. KTU questions often give this case in the truth table — students must mark it as **"Invalid" or "Not Allowed"**, not "no change".

## 2.2 Why "Asynchronous"?

The word *asynchronous* means *"not synchronous"*, i.e., **not time-aligned with the clock**. The synchronous inputs (D, J, K, T) are sampled only at the **active clock edge** (rising or falling, depending on the device). The asynchronous inputs:

- Do **not** require a clock edge to take effect.
- Act as soon as the input combination is recognized at the gate level.
- Override the synchronous inputs entirely (the clocked D/J/K/T lines are effectively ignored).
- Affect Q with a delay determined only by the **propagation delay of the asynchronous path** (not the clock-to-Q delay).

## 2.3 Why "Active LOW"?

Most flip-flops use **active-LOW** asynchronous inputs because:

1. **Noise immunity** — a default HIGH (inactive) state is safer in TTL/CMOS logic.
2. **Wired-OR capability** — multiple devices can share a common clear line (open-collector outputs).
3. **Historical convention** — the JK master-slave flip-flop (74LS73, 74LS76) and the D flip-flop (74LS74, 74LS174) all standardized on active-LOW direct inputs.

## 2.4 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous — Operational Comparison

| Property | Synchronous Inputs (D, J, K) | Asynchronous Inputs (PRE, CLR) |
|---|---|---|
| Wait for clock edge? | **YES** | **NO** |
| Affected by setup/hold time? | **YES** | **NO** |
| Override clocked behavior? | **NO** | **YES** |
| Used for initialization? | Not directly | **YES** (power-on clear) |
| Used for power-on reset? | No | **YES** |
| Typical use | Data transfer, counting | System reset, loading initial state |

## 2.5 Asynchronous Active-HIGH Convention

Some flip-flops (e.g., 74LS109 JK flip-flop) use **active-HIGH** asynchronous inputs. The logic simply **inverts**: asserting PRE = **1** forces Q to 1, asserting CLR = **1** forces Q to 0. Always **check the bubble** on the schematic symbol or the manufacturer's datasheet to determine polarity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Convention:** The KTU syllabus (Mano's *Digital Design* chapter on flip-flops) uses **active-LOW** asynchronous inputs as the default convention. Unless explicitly stated otherwise, assume $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$.

## 2.6 The Metastability Problem (Advanced)

When an asynchronous input signal is released very close to an active clock edge, the flip-flop's internal latch can enter a **metastable state** — an undefined voltage level between logic 0 and logic 1 that resolves randomly after a short time $t_r$ (resolution time).

### 2.6.1 The MTBF Equation

The **Mean Time Between Failures** due to metastability is given by:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{C_2 \cdot t_r}}{C_1 \cdot f_{CLK} \cdot f_{DATA}}
$$

where:
- $f_{CLK}$ = clock frequency of the receiving system
- $f_{DATA}$ = asynchronous event rate (rate of change of the asynchronous input)
- $t_r$ = allowed resolution time (time after clock edge before the output must be valid)
- $C_1$ = device-dependent constant (related to input slew rate)
- $C_2$ = device-dependent constant (related to internal gain)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** To minimize metastability failures, **double-flop synchronizers** are used. Two cascaded D flip-flops in series reduce the metastability probability exponentially because the second stage has a full clock period to resolve.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_{pd,\text{async}}$ | Asynchronous-to-Q propagation delay | 2 – 10 | ns |
| $t_{clk \to Q}$ | Clock-to-Q delay | 5 – 15 | ns |
| $t_{rec}$ | Recovery time (async inactive → clock edge) | 5 – 25 | ns |
| $t_{rem}$ | Removal time (async inactive ↔ clock edge) | 5 – 20 | ns |
| $t_s$ | Setup time (synchronous input) | 3 – 20 | ns |
| $t_h$ | Hold time (synchronous input) | 0 – 5 | ns |
| $\text{MTBF}$ | Mean time between metastability failures | $\frac{e^{C_2 t_r}}{C_1 f_{CLK} f_{DATA}}$ | seconds |
| $C_1$ | Metastability constant 1 | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-1}$ (device dep.) | dimensionless |
| $C_2$ | Metastability constant 2 | $10^{9}$ – $10^{12}$ (device dep.) | 1/s |
| $t_r$ | Allowed metastability resolution time | $T_{CLK} - t_{setup}$ | s |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Power-On Reset (POR) circuits** — assert CLR to initialize all flip-flops to a known state at startup.
2. **Watchdog timers** — when the system hangs, an asynchronous CLR forces a restart.
3. **Emergency stop** — a hardwired CLR overrides normal clocked logic to halt machinery.
4. **Memory loading** — PRE is used to load initial test vectors or boot-up codes.
5. **DMA handshaking** — asynchronous flags in peripheral interfaces.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Asynchronous Truth Table of a JK Flip-Flop

The standard JK flip-flop (active-LOW asynchronous inputs) has the following control logic. The internal SR latch is gated by the asynchronous inputs first, then by the clocked JK logic.

### Step 1 — Identify the Asynchronous Path

The asynchronous inputs PRE and CLR feed the internal NAND gates that set/reset the output latch **directly**, regardless of the clock:

$$
Q_{\text{next}} = \overline{\overline{CLR} \cdot (Q_{\text{internal}} \cdot \overline{PRE})}
$$

When $\overline{PRE} = 0$ (asserted):
$$
Q_{\text{next}} = 1, \quad \overline{Q}_{\text{next}} = 0
$$

When $\overline{CLR} = 0$ (asserted):
$$
Q_{\text{next}} = 0, \quad \overline{Q}_{\text{next}} = 1
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Synchronous Path

When both asynchronous inputs are inactive ($\overline{PRE} = 1$ and $\overline{CLR} = 1$), the flip-flop operates as a normal JK flip-flop on the active clock edge (falling edge for 74LS73, rising edge for 74LS76):

$$
Q_{\text{next}} = J \cdot \overline{Q} + \overline{K} \cdot Q
$$

### Step 3 — Assemble the Full Truth Table

| $\overline{PRE}$ | $\overline{CLR}$ | CLK | J | K | Q (next) | $\overline{Q}$ (next) | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | X | X | X | 1 | 0 | **Asynchronous Preset** |
| 1 | 0 | X | X | X | 0 | 1 | **Asynchronous Clear** |
| 0 | 0 | X | X | X | 1 | 1 | **Invalid** |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 0 | 0 | Q | $\overline{Q}$ | No change |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Reset |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 1 | 1 | $\overline{Q}$ | Q | **Toggle** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The "X" entries for J and K when the asynchronous input is active are crucial. KTU expects you to mark them as **"don't care"** (X) because the asynchronous input overrides the synchronous inputs.

## 3.2 Deriving the Asynchronous Truth Table of a D Flip-Flop

The D flip-flop (active-LOW asynchronous inputs) follows a similar pattern.

### Step 1 — Synchronous Operation

When $\overline{PRE} = 1$ and $\overline{CLR} = 1$, on the active clock edge:
$$
Q_{\text{next}} = D
$$

### Step 2 — Asynchronous Operation

Asynchronous inputs override:

$$
Q_{\text{next}} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } \overline{PRE} = 0 \text{ (and } \overline{CLR} = 1 \text{)} \\
0 & \text{if } \overline{CLR} = 0 \text{ (and } \overline{PRE} = 1 \text{)} \\
D & \text{on active clock edge, if } \overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 1 \\
\text{Invalid} & \text{if } \overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 0
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Full Truth Table

| $\overline{PRE}$ | $\overline{CLR}$ | CLK | D | Q (next) | $\overline{Q}$ (next) | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | X | X | 1 | 0 | Asynchronous Preset |
| 1 | 0 | X | X | 0 | 1 | Asynchronous Clear |
| 0 | 0 | X | X | 1 | 1 | **Invalid** |
| 1 | 1 | $\uparrow$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | Synchronous Reset |
| 1 | 1 | $\uparrow$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | Synchronous Set |

## 3.3 Deriving the Metastability MTBF Equation

### Step 1 — Definition of MTBF

The Mean Time Between Failures is the expected time between two metastability events that propagate to the system:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\text{Mean failure rate}}
$$

### Step 2 — Failure Rate Expression

The probability that a flip-flop enters metastability in a given clock cycle (when an asynchronous event coincides with the clock edge) is:

$$
P_{\text{metastable}} = C_1 \cdot f_{CLK} \cdot f_{DATA}
$$

where $C_1$ is a constant (with units of time) determined by the device's input circuitry and noise characteristics.

### Step 3 — Resolution Probability

The probability that the metastable state has **not** resolved by time $t_r$ (the allowed settling time) follows an exponential decay:

$$
P_{\text{unresolved}}(t_r) = e^{-C_2 \cdot t_r}
$$

where $C_2$ is a device-dependent constant (with units of $1/\text{time}$) representing the internal gain-bandwidth product of the latch's feedback loop.

### Step 4 — Combining for MTBF

The total failure rate is the product of the metastable-entry probability and the unresolved probability. The MTBF is the inverse:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{P_{\text{metastable}} \cdot P_{\text{unresolved}}}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{C_1 \cdot f_{CLK} \cdot f_{DATA} \cdot e^{-C_2 \cdot t_r}}
$$

Rearranging the exponential into the numerator:

$$
\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{C_2 \cdot t_r}}{C_1 \cdot f_{CLK} \cdot f_{DATA}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example

**Given:** A 74LS74 D flip-flop has $C_1 = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ s, $C_2 = 4.6 \times 10^{9}$ s$^{-1}$, clock frequency $f_{CLK} = 10$ MHz, asynchronous event rate $f_{DATA} = 1$ MHz, and $t_r = 20$ ns.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{(4.6 \times 10^9)(20 \times 10^{-9})}}{(4 \times 10^{-4})(10 \times 10^6)(1 \times 10^6)}
$$

Compute the exponent:

$$
C_2 \cdot t_r = (4.6 \times 10^9)(20 \times 10^{-9}) = 4.6 \times 20 = 92
$$

Compute the exponential:

$$
e^{92} \approx 9.7 \times 10^{39}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
C_1 \cdot f_{CLK} \cdot f_{DATA} = (4 \times 10^{-4})(10^7)(10^6) = 4 \times 10^9
$$

Compute the MTBF:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{9.7 \times 10^{39}}{4 \times 10^9} = 2.4 \times 10^{30} \text{ s}
$$

This is approximately **7.6 × 10²² years** — effectively never failing in the device's lifetime.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Active-HIGH vs. Active-LOW

| Criterion | Active-LOW ($\overline{PRE}$, $\overline{CLR}$) | Active-HIGH (PRE, CLR) |
|---|---|---|
| Default state | Inactive HIGH — safer for power-on | Inactive LOW — requires pull-down |
| Noise immunity | Better (default HIGH) | Worse (default LOW) |
| Wired-OR capability | Yes (open-collector) | No |
| Common in modern ICs | Yes (74LS, 74HC, 74FCT families) | Rare (specialty parts) |
| KTU exam convention | **Default** | Only when explicitly stated |

## 3.5 Derivation of Recovery and Removal Time Constraints

### Recovery Time Derivation

The recovery time $t_{rec}$ is derived from the requirement that **after deasserting the asynchronous input**, the flip-flop's internal control logic must return to a stable state before the next active clock edge. If the clock edge arrives sooner than $t_{rec}$ after deassertion, the flip-flop may fail to respond to the clock correctly.

**Constraint:**

$$
t_{\text{async-release}} + t_{rec} \leq t_{\text{next-active-edge}}
$$

That is, the time from the deassertion of the asynchronous input to the next active clock edge must be at least $t_{rec}$.

### Removal Time Derivation

The removal time $t_{rem}$ is the symmetric requirement on the **other side**: the asynchronous input must be deasserted at least $t_{rem}$ **before** the next active clock edge. If the asynchronous input is removed closer to the clock edge than $t_{rem}$, the flip-flop's behavior is undefined.

**Constraint:**

$$
t_{\text{async-release}} \geq t_{\text{prev-active-edge}} + t_{rem}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined constraint** for reliable operation:
> $$ t_{\text{prev-edge}} + t_{rem} \leq t_{\text{async-release}} \leq t_{\text{next-edge}} - t_{rec} $$

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Asynchronous Input Path vs. Clocked Input Path

The diagram below illustrates the internal architecture of an edge-triggered D flip-flop with asynchronous Preset and Clear. Notice how the asynchronous inputs **bypass** the master-slave clocked section and feed the output latch directly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D["D Input"] --> G1
    CLK["CLK"] --> G1
    G1["Master Latch (Clocked)"] --> G2
    G2["Slave Latch (Clocked)"] --> Qout["Q Output"]
    G2 --> Qbarout["Q-bar Output"]
    PRE["PRE-bar (Asynchronous)"] --> ASYNC["Async Control Logic"]
    CLR["CLR-bar (Asynchronous)"] --> ASYNC
    ASYNC -.->|"Overrides directly"| G1
    ASYNC -.->|"Overrides directly"| G2
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **clocked path** (solid arrows) flows D → Master Latch → Slave Latch → Q, gated by CLK.
- The **asynchronous path** (dashed arrows) jumps from the async control logic directly into both latches.
- The async inputs effectively **short-circuit** the clocked path when active.

## 4.2 State Machine: Asynchronous Input Arbitration

The following flowchart captures the **decision priority** of the asynchronous control logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start of cycle"] --> B0{"PRE-bar = 0?"}
    B0 -->|"Yes"| C0["Q = 1, Q-bar = 0 (Preset)"]
    B0 -->|"No"| D0{"CLR-bar = 0?"}
    D0 -->|"Yes"| E0["Q = 0, Q-bar = 1 (Clear)"]
    D0 -->|"No"| F0{"Clock active edge?"}
    F0 -->|"Yes"| G0["Apply synchronous logic (D, JK, T)"]
    F0 -->|"No"| H0["Q holds previous state"]
    C0 --> Z0["End"]
    E0 --> Z0["End"]
    G0 --> Z0["End"]
    H0 --> Z0["End"]
```

## 4.3 Timing Diagram: D Flip-Flop with Asynchronous Clear

The following Gantt-style diagram shows the behavior of a D flip-flop with an active-LOW asynchronous CLR. The CLR pulse asynchronously forces Q to 0, ignoring both the clock and the D input. When CLR returns HIGH, normal clocked operation resumes.

```mermaid
gantt
    title D Flip-Flop Timing: Asynchronous CLR Pulse
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section CLK
    CLK_low    :a1, 0, 5
    CLK_high   :a2, 5, 10
    CLK_low2   :a3, 10, 15
    CLK_high2  :a4, 15, 20
    CLK_low3   :a5, 20, 25
    CLK_high3  :a6, 25, 30
    
    section CLR-bar
    CLR_high_a  :b1, 0, 8
    CLR_low     :b2, 8, 14
    CLR_high_b  :b3, 14, 30
    
    section D
    D_high     :c1, 0, 30
    
    section Q
    Q_a        :d1, 0, 8
    Q_zero     :crit, d2, 8, 14
    Q_high_b   :d3, 14, 30
```

**Reading the timing diagram:**
- From t = 0 to t = 8, $\overline{CLR}$ = 1 (inactive) and Q follows D normally on clock edges.
- At t = 8, $\overline{CLR}$ falls to 0 (active). **Immediately**, Q is forced to 0 — without waiting for a clock edge.
- At t = 14, $\overline{CLR}$ returns to 1. On the next active clock edge, Q resumes following D.

## 4.4 Double-Flop Synchronizer Architecture

The canonical solution to the metastability hazard in asynchronous input handling is a **two-stage synchronizer**. The first flip-flop may enter a metastable state; by the time the second flip-flop samples its output, the metastability has resolved.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ASYNC_IN["Asynchronous Input (External)"] --> FF1
    FF1["D-FF Stage 1 (May be metastable)"] -->|"After 1 clock period"| FF2
    FF2["D-FF Stage 2 (Metastability resolved)"] --> SYNC_OUT["Synchronized Output to System Clock Domain"]
    CLK1["System CLK"] --> FF1
    CLK1 --> FF2
```

**Why two stages?**
- The first flip-flop is the "sacrificial" stage: if metastability occurs, it has **one full clock period** to resolve.
- The second flip-flop samples the (now stable) output of the first, producing a clean synchronized signal.
- The probability of both stages being metastable simultaneously is **exponentially smaller** (by a factor of $e^{C_2 T_{CLK}}$).

## 4.5 Asynchronous Active-LOW Asynchronous Symbol Convention

```
            ┌─────────────┐
    D  ────►│             │──► Q
            │   D-FF      │
    CLK ───►│  (positive  │──► Q-bar
            │    edge)    │
            │             │
  PRE-bar ─►│◉            │
  (preset)  │  Async Set  │
            │             │
  CLR-bar ─►│◉            │
  (clear)   │ Async Reset │
            └─────────────┘
```

The bubbles `◉` on PRE and CLR lines indicate **active-LOW** inputs. Without the bubble, the input would be active-HIGH.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What are asynchronous inputs in a flip-flop? Why are they called "asynchronous"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Asynchronous inputs (typically Preset $\overline{PRE}$ and Clear $\overline{CLR}$) are special flip-flop inputs that **change the output state independently of the clock signal**. They are called "asynchronous" because their action is **not synchronized with the clock** — they can set or reset the flip-flop at any instant, overriding the synchronous data inputs. They are implemented as **active-LOW** inputs in most standard ICs (e.g., 74LS74, 74LS76), asserting a logic 0 to force the output.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous inputs of a JK flip-flop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Synchronous (J, K) | Asynchronous ($\overline{PRE}$, $\overline{CLR}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Clock dependence | Require active clock edge | Independent of clock |
| Override behavior | Cannot override clocked inputs | Override clocked inputs |
| Setup/hold time | Must be satisfied | Not applicable |
| Primary use | Data operations (set/reset/toggle) | System initialization, emergency reset |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Design and explain the operation of a JK flip-flop with active-LOW asynchronous Preset and Clear inputs. Draw the complete truth table including all input combinations. Discuss what happens when both $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$ are asserted simultaneously.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Draw the circuit and explain the asynchronous path.**

**Model Answer:**

The JK flip-flop with active-LOW asynchronous inputs is constructed by adding two **NAND gates** to the clocked JK master-slave flip-flop. The asynchronous inputs are applied to the NAND gates that form the cross-coupled output latch, allowing them to **bypass the clocked section** entirely.

**Circuit Description:**

1. The synchronous JK logic produces internal signals $S'$ and $R'$ based on J, K, Q, and $\overline{Q}$.
2. These signals feed the input NAND gates of the output latch.
3. The asynchronous inputs $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$ also feed the same input NAND gates **directly**.
4. When either asynchronous input is active (logic 0), it **forces** the corresponding NAND output HIGH, which then sets or resets the output latch.
5. The clocked $S'$ and $R'$ signals are **overridden** by the asynchronous path.

**Asynchronous Truth Table Subset:**

| $\overline{PRE}$ | $\overline{CLR}$ | CLK | J | K | Q (next) | $\overline{Q}$ (next) | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | X | X | X | 1 | 0 | Asynchronous Preset |
| 1 | 0 | X | X | X | 0 | 1 | Asynchronous Clear |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 0 | 0 | Q | $\overline{Q}$ | No change |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | Reset |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | Set |
| 1 | 1 | $\downarrow$ | 1 | 1 | $\overline{Q}$ | Q | Toggle |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the $\overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 0$ condition and discuss recovery time.**

**Model Answer:**

**Simultaneous Assertion of $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$:**

When both $\overline{PRE} = 0$ and $\overline{CLR} = 0$ simultaneously, the internal NAND gates are forced to produce **HIGH at both inputs of the cross-coupled latch**. This drives both Q and $\overline{Q}$ to logic 1 — a state that violates the **fundamental complementarity** requirement of a flip-flop (Q and $\overline{Q}$ should always be opposite). This is called the **invalid** or **forbidden** state.

When both asynchronous inputs are simultaneously released (return to 1), the flip-flop enters a **race condition**: which NAND gate responds first determines whether Q settles to 0 or 1. The outcome is **unpredictable** and depends on:
- Internal gate propagation delay mismatches
- Temperature
- Supply voltage variations
- Manufacturing tolerances

This is why KTU exam questions always classify the $\overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 0$ case as **"Invalid"** or **"Not allowed"** — it must be **avoided** in any practical design.

**Recovery Time Discussion:**

After $\overline{PRE}$ or $\overline{CLR}$ is deasserted (returns to 1), the internal control logic requires a finite time to **return to a stable state** before the next active clock edge is applied. This minimum time is the **recovery time $t_{rec}$**.

If the next active clock edge arrives **before** $t_{rec}$ has elapsed, the flip-flop may:
- Fail to respond to the clocked inputs
- Enter a metastable state
- Produce an unpredictable output

**Design rule:**

$$
t_{\text{clock edge}} - t_{\text{async deassertion}} \geq t_{rec}
$$

For 74LS74, $t_{rec} \approx 5$ ns. For 74HC74, $t_{rec} \approx 3$ ns. Designers ensure this constraint by either:
- Stretching the asynchronous pulse to be long enough
- Using clock gating to suppress the next clock edge during recovery
- Using a synchronizer (Section 4.4) when the asynchronous input is truly asynchronous

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: With the help of a neat timing diagram, explain the operation of a D flip-flop having active-LOW asynchronous Preset and Clear inputs. Assume a specific input sequence and show how the asynchronous inputs override the clocked behavior. Also discuss the metastability hazard that arises when an asynchronous input is released near a clock edge.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Draw and explain the timing diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumed Input Sequence:**

| Time (ns) | CLK | D | $\overline{PRE}$ | $\overline{CLR}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 20 | 0 (initial) | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 20 – 40 | $\uparrow$ then 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 40 – 60 | $\downarrow$ then 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 60 – 80 | $\uparrow$ then 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 80 – 100 | $\downarrow$ then 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Step-by-step analysis of Q:**

- **t = 0 to 20:** $\overline{CLR} = 0$ (asserted) → Q = 0 asynchronously, regardless of D. CLK and D are ignored.
- **t = 20:** CLK rising edge with D = 1. Since $\overline{CLR} = 0$ still asserted, Q remains 0 (asynchronous overrides).
- **t = 20 to 40:** $\overline{CLR}$ returns to 1 at t = 40. During this interval, CLK = 1, but no active edge occurs. Q stays 0.
- **t = 40:** $\overline{PRE}$ asserted (falls to 0) → Q goes to 1 **immediately**, no clock edge needed.
- **t = 60:** $\overline{PRE}$ returns to 1. Now $\overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 1$ (both inactive), so synchronous operation resumes.
- **t = 60 to 80:** CLK rising edge at t = 60 with D = 0 → Q = 0.
- **t = 80 to 100:** CLK rising edge with D = 1 → Q = 1.

**Sketching the timing diagram (textual representation):**

```
Time →     0    20   40   60   80  100
CLK:      ___|‾‾|___|‾‾‾‾|___|‾‾‾‾|___
D:        ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
PRE-bar:  ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___________|‾‾‾‾‾
CLR-bar:  |‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
Q:        _______|‾‾‾‾|_______|‾‾‾‾
```

**Key observation:** Notice how Q changes **only in response to** either an asynchronous input transition or a clock edge — and asynchronous transitions occur **without** a clock edge.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss metastability hazard and its mitigation.**

**Model Answer:**

**The Metastability Hazard:**

When an asynchronous input (say $\overline{CLR}$) is **deasserted very close to an active clock edge** (i.e., within the recovery/removal time window $t_{rec}$ or $t_{rem}$), the flip-flop's internal cross-coupled latch may be **caught in an intermediate state** where both Q and $\overline{Q}$ are at the same voltage level — neither a clean 0 nor a clean 1. This is the **metastable state**.

The flip-flop will eventually resolve to a stable state (either 0 or 1) after a random time $t_r$, governed by an exponential distribution:

$$
P(\text{resolution time} > t_r) = e^{-C_2 \cdot t_r}
$$

If the system samples Q before resolution occurs, it reads an **undefined value**, causing a logic error in the downstream circuit.

**When does metastability occur?**

- Asynchronous input deassertion within $t_{rec}$ of the next clock edge
- Asynchronous data being sampled on a clock edge (e.g., external interrupt signals entering a synchronous system)

**Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Two-Stage Synchronizer (Double-Flop):**
   - The asynchronous input feeds a D flip-flop clocked by the system clock.
   - The output of that flip-flop feeds a second D flip-flop.
   - The first flip-flop is allowed to be metastable; by the time the second flip-flop samples (one full clock period later), the metastability has typically resolved.

2. **Clock Gating:**
   - Suppress the system clock for one or more cycles after an asynchronous event is detected, giving the flip-flop extra time to recover.

3. **Faster Flip-Flops:**
   - Flip-flops with higher internal gain-bandwidth product (higher $C_2$) recover from metastability faster.

4. **MTBF Engineering:**
   - Use the MTBF equation to ensure the system tolerates the expected asynchronous event rate:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{C_2 \cdot t_r}}{C_1 \cdot f_{CLK} \cdot f_{DATA}}
$$

   - For a target MTBF (e.g., 10 years), solve for the required $t_r$ and add synchronizer stages accordingly.

5. **Avoiding Simultaneous Asynchronous Assertion:**
   - In practical designs, never assert $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$ simultaneously. Use priority logic (e.g., external gating) to ensure only one is active at any time.

**Conclusion:** Metastability cannot be eliminated entirely, but its probability can be reduced to arbitrarily small values using synchronizers and disciplined asynchronous input management. This is a critical design concern in any system crossing clock domains.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Asynchronous Input questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "X" (don't care) entries** in the truth table for J, K, D, and CLK when an asynchronous input is active. Marks are docked if you assume the synchronous inputs still matter.
> 2. **Mis-classifying the $\overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 0$ state.** Do NOT write "no change" or "Q = 0". The correct answer is **"Invalid"** or **"Not Allowed"**. Examiners explicitly test this.
