> 3. **Confusing synchronous and asynchronous reset.** In a VHDL/Verilog design, `reset` (in a process sensitivity list) is **synchronous**, while `preset`/`clear` outside the clock-edge process are **asynchronous**. KTU questions may ask this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting the bubble** on the asynchronous input symbol. If the schematic shows $\overline{PRE}$ with a bubble, the active level is LOW. A common error is asserting HIGH and expecting a preset.
> 5. **Omitting recovery time discussion.** A 7-mark question on asynchronous inputs almost always expects a paragraph on $t_{rec}$ and metastability. Skipping it loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Drawing the timing diagram with clock-edge alignment for asynchronous transitions.** Asynchronous transitions must be drawn as **sloped lines** that occur independently of the clock, NOT aligned to any clock edge.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Asynchronous Inputs**

- **Definition:** Asynchronous inputs (Preset and Clear) change the flip-flop output **without** waiting for a clock edge.
- **Default Polarity:** **Active-LOW** in standard ICs (74LS74, 74LS76, 74HC74); a logic **0** asserts the input.
- **Truth Table — 4 Key Rows:**
  - $\overline{PRE} = 0$, $\overline{CLR} = 1$ → **Q = 1** (Preset)
  - $\overline{PRE} = 1$, $\overline{CLR} = 0$ → **Q = 0** (Clear)
  - $\overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 0$ → **Invalid** (Q and $\overline{Q}$ both forced to 1, then race on release)
  - $\overline{PRE} = \overline{CLR} = 1$ → Normal **clocked** operation
- **Override Behavior:** Asynchronous inputs **always** override synchronous inputs (D, J, K, T) when active.
- **Key Timing Parameters:**
  - $t_{rec}$ (recovery time): minimum gap between async deassertion and next clock edge
  - $t_{rem}$ (removal time): minimum gap between previous clock edge and async deassertion
  - Asynchronous path delay $t_{pd,\text{async}}$ is **independent** of clock
- **Metastability:**
  - Occurs when async input is released near a clock edge (within $t_{rec}$/$t_{rem}$)
  - Probability of unresolved metastable state: $e^{-C_2 t_r}$
  - **MTBF formula:** $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{C_2 t_r}}{C_1 f_{CLK} f_{DATA}}$
  - **Mitigation:** Two-stage (double-flop) synchronizer
- **Applications:** Power-on reset, watchdog timers, emergency stop, system initialization, memory loading
- **Common ICs:**
  - 74LS74 / 74HC74 — Dual D flip-flop with $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$
  - 74LS76 / 74HC76 — Dual JK flip-flop with $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$
  - 74LS109 / 74HC109 — Dual JK flip-flop with active-HIGH $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$ (check datasheet!)
- **Schematic Symbol:** Look for the **bubble `◉`** on the async input pin to confirm active-LOW.
- **Golden Rule:** Never assert both $\overline{PRE}$ and $\overline{CLR}$ simultaneously in a real circuit — the result is undefined.
- **Double-Flop Synchronizer Rule of Thumb:** Always cascade two D flip-flops when bringing an external asynchronous signal into a synchronous clock domain. This is the **industry standard** for CDC (Clock Domain Crossing).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronizer Failure and Metastability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT416 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Synchronizer Failure and Metastability # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Metastability** is an undefined logical state that a bistable storage element (flip-flop or latch) may enter when its setup time ($t_{su}$) or hold time ($t_h$) constraints are violated. In this metastable state, the output hovers at an indeterminate voltage level between logic 0 and logic 1 for an unpredictable, theoretically unbounded duration before eventually resolving to a stable state. **Synchronizer Failure** occurs when a synchronizer circuit, intended to safely transfer an asynchronous signal into a synchronous clock domain, fails to resolve metastability within the available time budget, propagating an undefined logic value into the rest of the synchronous system and potentially causing catastrophic functional errors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchors:** > - **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** The minimum time the data input must remain stable **BEFORE** the active clock edge. > - **Hold Time ($t_h$):** The minimum time the data input must remain stable **AFTER** the active clock edge. > - **Resolution Time ($t_r$):** The time required for a metastable output to settle to a valid logic level. > - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average time interval between successive metastability-induced system failures. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaning Tower of Dominoes" Imagine a perfectly balanced domino standing perfectly upright on its narrow edge. The domino is **bistable** — it can fall either to the LEFT (logic 0) or to the RIGHT (logic 1). This is exactly how a flip-flop stores a bit. Now, suppose you push the domino at the **exact instant** another domino is supposed to topple it. The forces collide. The domino **wobbles** — neither falling left nor right. It just **vibrates** at the edge for a fraction of a second before the laws of physics and tiny imperfections in the surface decide its fate. That wobbling, trembling, indeterminate state is **metastability**. In digital electronics: - The "edge of the domino" = the sampling instant of a flip-flop. - The "push at the wrong time" = a data transition that violates setup or hold time. - The "wobble duration" = the **resolution time**. - The "imperfections deciding the fate" = thermal noise and device mismatches. > [!NOTE] > **Why Cannot Engineers Just "Fix" Metastability?** > Metastability is a **fundamental physical inevitability** in any bistable system with finite gain-bandwidth. It is mathematically proven (by Chavet & Manolescu in 1971) that no physical flip-flop can be 100% metastability-immune. Engineers can only **reduce its probability** and **bound its duration** using synchronizer circuits. ## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Examinations 1. Directly tests understanding of **asynchronous-to-synchronous domain crossing (CDC)**. 2. Requires application of the **MTBF formula** — a frequently asked numerical problem. 3. Tests knowledge of practical synchronizer architectures (1-flop, 2-flop, 3-flop, handshake). 4. Bridges theoretical digital electronics with real-world VLSI design failure modes. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Bistable Element — Origin of Metastability A flip-flop is fundamentally a **positive feedback amplifier** with two stable operating points: HIGH (logic 1) and LOW (logic 0). The transfer characteristic of this cross-coupled pair forms a hysteresis loop with two stable equilibria. When the input data transitions **well before** the clock edge, the flip-flop cleanly enters one of the two stable states with full noise margin. When the data transitions **exactly at** the clock edge, the feedback loop's regenerative gain is forced to operate in its **linear region** rather than the saturation region. The output then "rests" on the unstable equilibrium point of the hysteresis curve — a point where the derivative of the transfer function equals 1, making the system **marginally stable**. > [!NOTE] > **Regenerative Time Constant ($\tau$):** The time constant of the positive feedback loop determines how fast the metastable state decays. Typical CMOS flip-flops have $\tau$ in the range of **200 ps to 500 ps**. ## 2.2 The Metastability Resolution Process Once a flip-flop enters metastability, the output voltage $V_{out}(t)$ follows an exponential decay from the metastable point $V_M$ toward one of the two stable rails: $$V_{out}(t) = V_M + \Delta V_0 \cdot e^{(t - t_0)/\tau}$$ Where: - $V_M \approx V_{DD}/2$ is the metastable equilibrium voltage. - $\Delta V_0$ is the initial perturbation (a function of thermal noise). - $\tau$ is the regeneration time constant. - $t_0$ is the moment of metastable entry. The **probability** that the output has NOT resolved by time $T$ (i.e., the system is still metastable at time $T$) is given by: $$P(\text{not resolved at time } T) = e^{-(T - t_0)/\tau}$$ This exponential dependence is why the **MTBF increases dramatically** as the available resolution time $T$ increases. ## 2.3 The Synchronizer Concept A **synchronizer** is a circuit placed between an asynchronous source and a synchronous destination that provides a deterministic delay window during which metastability can resolve. The simplest and most widely used is the **two-flop synchronizer**. ### Two-Flop Synchronizer Operation: 1. **First Flip-Flop (FF1):** Samples the asynchronous input directly. May enter metastability, but its output is **isolated** from the rest of the system. 2. **Second Flip-Flop (FF2):** Samples FF1's output one full clock cycle later. By this time, metastability in FF1 has almost certainly resolved to a valid logic level. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The two-flop synchronizer does NOT eliminate metastability — it merely **contains** it within FF1 and provides a full clock period for resolution. The probability of failure is dramatically reduced but never zero. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | |---|---------------------|---------------|---------------------| | 1 | **MTBF Equation** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{t_r/\tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$ | Mean time between metastability failures | | 2 | Resolution Probability | $P(t > t_r) = e^{-t_r/\tau}$ | Probability state unresolved after time $t_r$ | | 3 | Setup Time Bound | $t_{su} \leq t_{data,arrival} - t_{clk,edge}$ | Constraint for clean capture | | 4 | Hold Time Bound | $t_h \leq t_{data,arrival} - t_{clk,edge}$ | Constraint against early data change | | 5 | Available Resolution Time (2-flop) | $t_r = T_{clk} - t_{cq,FF1} - t_{su,FF2}$ | Time window for metastability to decay | | 6 | Asynchronous Data Rate | $f_{async} = f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}$ | Effective arrival frequency | | 7 | Metastability Time Constant | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{G \cdot B}$ | Inverse of loop gain-bandwidth product | | 8 | Failure Rate (per second) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Reciprocal of MTBF | **Where:** - $T_0$ = device-specific metastability constant (typically 0.1 to 100 seconds for modern CMOS). - $f_{clk}$ = sampling clock frequency of the synchronizer. - $f_{data}$ = rate of asynchronous data transitions. - $t_r$ = available resolution time after the clock edge. - $\tau$ = metastability regeneration time constant. - $G \cdot B$ = gain-bandwidth product of the feedback loop. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility 1. **Multi-Clock Domain SoC Design:** In chips with 20+ clock domains, CDC synchronizers are mandatory in every cross-domain path. 2. **FPGA Design:** Vendor tools (Xilinx, Intel/Altera) provide built-in synchronizer primitives (e.g., Xilinx `ASYNC_REG` attribute) whose MTBF is documented in datasheets. 3. **Network Equipment:** High-speed serial links (PCIe, Ethernet, USB) use elastic buffers and synchronizers to handle clock domain crossings between recovered clocks and system clocks. 4. **Aerospace & Medical Electronics:** Safety-critical systems must demonstrate a target MTBF (often $> 10^{12}$ years) to meet certification standards. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the MTBF Formula ### Step 1: Define the Rate of Metastable Events A metastable event occurs when an asynchronous data transition falls within the **metastability aperture** — a small time window of width $a$ centered on the clock edge. The probability per clock cycle of an asynchronous transition landing inside this aperture is: $$P_{\text{event per cycle}} = a \cdot f_{data}$$ ### Step 2: Define the Probability That the Event Fails If the event occurs, the synchronizer fails only if the metastability has NOT resolved by the time FF2 captures. This probability is: $$P_{\text{fail}} = e^{-t_r/\tau}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Rate of Failures Per Second The system samples at frequency $f_{clk}$. The failure rate per second is: $$\lambda = f_{clk} \cdot P_{\text{event per cycle}} \cdot P_{\text{fail}}$$ Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2: $$\lambda = f_{clk} \cdot (a \cdot f_{data}) \cdot e^{-t_r/\tau}$$ ### Step 4: Absorb Device Constants Group all device-specific constants ($a$ and the inverse of any proportionality) into a single parameter $T_0$: $$T_0 = \dfrac{1}{a \cdot (\text{device constant})}$$ This gives the clean form: $$\lambda = \dfrac{f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}{T_0} \cdot e^{-t_r/\tau}$$ ### Step 5: Take the Reciprocal to Get MTBF $$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{T_0 \cdot e^{t_r/\tau}}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$$ This is the canonical **MTBF equation** used in industry and tested in KTU examinations. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem) ### **Problem Statement:** A digital system uses a 2-flop synchronizer to sample an asynchronous signal. The following parameters are given: - Sampling clock frequency: $f_{clk} = 100 \text{ MHz}$ - Asynchronous data toggle rate: $f_{data} = 10 \text{ MHz}$ - Metastability time constant: $\tau = 350 \text{ ps}$ - Available resolution time: $t_r = 8 \text{ ns}$ - Device constant: $T_0 = 10 \text{ seconds}$ **Calculate:** (a) The probability that metastability persists beyond $t_r$. (b) The MTBF of the synchronizer in years. ### **Solution:** **Part (a): Probability of unresolved metastability** $$P(t > t_r) = e^{-t_r/\tau}$$ Substituting the given values: $$P(t > t_r) = e^{-(8 \times 10^{-9}) / (350 \times 10^{-12})}$$ The exponent evaluates as: $$t_r/\tau = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-9}}{3.5 \times 10^{-10}} = 22.857$$ Therefore: $$P(t > t_r) = e^{-22.857} \approx 1.24 \times 10^{-10}$$ **Part (b): Mean Time Between Failures** $$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_0 \cdot e^{t_r/\tau}}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}$$ Computing the exponential first: $$e^{t_r/\tau} = e^{22.857} \approx 8.05 \times 10^{9}$$ Substituting all values into the MTBF equation: $$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{10 \times 8.05 \times 10^{9}}{100 \times 10^{6} \times 10 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{8.05 \times 10^{10}}{10^{15}}$$ $$\text{MTBF} = 8.05 \times 10^{-5} \text{ seconds}$$ Converting to years (1 year = $3.15 \times 10^7$ seconds): $$\text{MTBF}_{years} = \dfrac{8.05 \times 10^{-5}}{3.15 \times 10^{7}} \approx 2.56 \times 10^{-12} \text{ years}$$ > [!WARNING] > **A negative exponent in MTBF years is NOT a math error!** It means the MTBF is FAR less than one second — the synchronizer is grossly inadequate. To fix this, either **increase $t_r$** (use a slower clock) or **add a third synchronizer flip-flop** to extend the resolution window. ### **Remediation: Adding a Third Flip-Flop** A 3-flop synchronizer provides an additional clock period of resolution time. With $t_r = 18 \text{ ns}$: $$e^{t_r/\tau} = e^{18 \times 10^{-9} / 3.5 \times 10^{-10}} = e^{51.43} \approx 1.55 \times 10^{22}$$ $$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{10 \times 1.55 \times 10^{22}}{10^{15}} = 1.55 \times 10^{8} \text{ seconds}$$ Converting to years: $$\text{MTBF}_{years} = \dfrac{1.55 \times 10^{8}}{3.15 \times 10^{7}} \approx 4.92 \text{ years}$$ This demonstrates the **exponential sensitivity** of MTBF to the available resolution time — a hallmark KTU 2024 examination point. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Synchronizer Architectures | Parameter | 1-Flop (Unsafe) | 2-Flop (Standard) | 3-Flop (High Reliability) | Handshake (Multi-bit) | |-----------|-----------------|-------------------|---------------------------|----------------------| | **MTBF (typical)** | Seconds to minutes | Years to decades | Centuries to millennia | Equivalent to 2-flop | | **Latency** | 1 clock cycle | 2 clock cycles | 3 clock cycles | Variable (request-ack) | | **Logic Area** | 1 FF | 2 FFs | 3 FFs | FFs + control logic | | **Use Case** | NEVER in production | General CDC | Aerospace, medical, automotive | Multi-bit buses | | **Resolution Time** | 0 (UNSAFE) | $T_{clk} - t_{su}$ | $2T_{clk} - t_{su}$ | $T_{clk} - t_{su}$ | | **Failure Mode** | Catastrophic | Probabilistic | Extremely rare | Handshake-locked | | **KTU Weightage** | Conceptual | High (numerical) | Medium | Definition-focused | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Two-Flop Synchronizer — Signal Flow Architecture ```mermaid graph LR AsyncInput["ASYNC INPUT SOURCE"] -->|asynchronous signal| FF1["D-FF 1
SYNCHRONIZER STAGE 1
Possible metastability zone"] FF1 -->|one clock period delay| FF2["D-FF 2
SYNCHRONIZER STAGE 2
Metastability resolved"] FF2 --> SyncOutput["SYNCHRONIZED OUTPUT
Safe for downstream logic"] ClkDomain["CLOCK DOMAIN CLK 100MHZ"] -->|clock edge| FF1 ClkDomain -->|clock edge| FF2 ``` ## 4.2 Metastability Resolution Time-Voltage Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A["Trigger Event
Data changes inside setup hold window"] --> B["Flip Flop enters metastable state
Output voltage hovers near VDD by 2"] B --> C["Exponential decay begins
Vout increases or decreases based on thermal noise"] C --> D{"Has resolution time t
elapsed"} D -->|YES| E["Output settles to VALID LOGIC 0 or LOGIC 1"] D -->|NO| F["Metastability persists
System failure occurs"] E --> G["Safe propagation to next stage"] F --> H["Undefined logic propagates
Synchronizer failure"] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3-Flop Synchronizer ```mermaid graph TB AsyncBus["ASYNCHRONOUS DATA BUS"] --> Stage1["SYNCHRONIZER FLIP FLOP 1
FF1
High metastability risk"] Stage1 -->|after 1 clock period| Stage2["SYNCHRONIZER FLIP FLOP 2
FF2
Resolution usually complete"] Stage2 -->|after 1 clock period| Stage3["SYNCHRONIZER FLIP FLOP 3
FF3
Final safety margin"] Stage3 --> SyncDomain["SYNCHRONOUS DOMAIN LOGIC
Fully safe consumption"] ClkSource["GLOBAL CLOCK"] --> Stage1 ClkSource --> Stage2 ClkSource --> Stage3 Stage1 -.->|metastable if violated| MetaNote["Set Async Reg attribute
in FPGA vendor tools"] ``` ## 4.4 Complete System-Level CDC Architecture ```mermaid graph LR DomainA["CLOCK DOMAIN A
FSource 50 MHz"] -->|asynchronous crossing| Sync1["TWO FLOP SYNCHRONIZER
Clocked in Domain B"] DomainB["CLOCK DOMAIN B
FClock 100 MHz"] --> Sync1 Sync1 --> Processing["DOMAIN B PROCESSING LOGIC"] Processing --> Sync2["MULTI FLOP SYNCHRONIZER
Handshake ready"] Sync2 --> DomainC["CLOCK DOMAIN C
FDest 200 MHz"] DomainC --> OutputLogic["OUTPUT LOGIC STAGE"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Examinations:** > - Arrows represent **data flow direction**. > - Boxes with clock labels are **sequential elements (flip-flops)**. > - Asynchronous crossings MUST always pass through a synchronizer block. > - The metastability zone is highlighted in the first synchronizer stage only. ## 4.5 Setup and Hold Time Violation Map ```mermaid graph TD ClockEdge["ACTIVE CLOCK EDGE"] --> SetupWindow["SETUP TIME WINDOW
Data must be stable BEFORE edge"] ClockEdge --> HoldWindow["HOLD TIME WINDOW
Data must be stable AFTER edge"] SetupWindow --> SafeCapture["CLEAN CAPTURE
No metastability risk"] HoldWindow --> SafeCapture SetupViolation["DATA TRANSITION INSIDE SETUP WINDOW"] --> MetastableEntry["FLIP FLOP ENTERS METASTABILITY"] HoldViolation["DATA TRANSITION INSIDE HOLD WINDOW"] --> MetastableEntry MetastableEntry --> ResolveDecay["EXPONENTIAL DECAY
Time constant tau"] ResolveDecay --> OutcomeCheck{"Resolution time t
exceeds decay requirement"} OutcomeCheck -->|Yes| ValidState["VALID LOGIC 0 OR 1"] OutcomeCheck -->|No| UndefinedState["UNDEFINED STATE PROPAGATION
System failure"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Metastability. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** Metastability is an undefined logical state in a bistable storage element (flip-flop or latch) that occurs when the input data violates the setup or hold time constraints relative to the active clock edge. In this state, the output voltage hovers at an intermediate level between logic 0 and logic 1 for an unpredictable, theoretically unbounded duration before eventually resolving to a stable state due to thermal noise and device asymmetries. Metastability is a fundamental physical phenomenon in all real bistable systems and **cannot be fully eliminated**, only managed through synchronizer circuits and proper timing margins. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory keywords for full 3 marks:** setup time violation, bistable, intermediate voltage, exponential decay, fundamental physical phenomenon. --- ### **Question 2: What is the role of a synchronizer in digital systems? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** A synchronizer is a circuit element placed between an asynchronous signal source and a synchronous destination that provides a deterministic delay window to allow any metastable state to resolve to a valid logic level before the signal is consumed by the synchronous system. The most common implementation is the **two-flop synchronizer**, which uses two cascaded flip-flops clocked in the destination domain. The first flip-flop may enter metastability, but the second flip-flop samples the first output one full clock cycle later — by which time metastability has almost certainly resolved. The synchronizer does not eliminate metastability; it merely **contains and bounds** its probability and duration. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Derive the MTBF equation for a metastable synchronizer. Define every symbol in the final expression.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify the metastability aperture** When an asynchronous data transition occurs within a small time window $a$ around the clock edge, the synchronizer may enter metastability. The probability of a data transition falling inside this aperture per clock cycle is: $$P_{\text{aperture}} = a \cdot f_{data}$$ **Step 2: Probability of failure given a metastable event** If metastability occurs, the system fails only if the metastable state has not resolved by the time the second flip-flop captures the data. The probability of unresolved metastability after time $t_r$ is exponential: $$P_{\text{unresolved}} = e^{-t_r/\tau}$$ **Step 3: Total failure rate per second** Multiplying the probability of an event by the probability of failure, and by the system sampling rate: $$\lambda = f_{clk} \cdot (a \cdot f_{data}) \cdot e^{-t_r/\tau}$$ **Step 4: Group device constants** Define $T_0 = 1/a \cdot (\text{device constant})$. The clean form becomes: $$\lambda = \dfrac{f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}{T_0} \cdot e^{-t_r/\tau}$$ **Step 5: Invert to get MTBF** $$\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_0 \cdot e^{t_r/\tau}}{f_{clk} \cdot f_{data}}}$$ **Symbol definitions:** - $T_0$: device-specific metastability constant (seconds). - $t_r$: available resolution time (seconds). - $\tau$: metastability regeneration time constant (seconds). - $f_{clk}$: sampling clock frequency (Hz). - $f_{data}$: asynchronous data toggle rate (Hz). **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A 2-flop synchronizer operates at 200 MHz clock frequency. The asynchronous data toggles at 50 MHz. Given $\tau = 250$ ps, $T_0 = 5$ seconds, and clock period $T_{clk} = 5$ ns with setup time $t_{su} = 0.5$ ns, calculate the MTBF in years and comment on its adequacy.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate available resolution time** $$t_r = T_{clk} - t_{su} = 5 \text{ ns} - 0.5 \text{ ns} = 4.5 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 2: Compute the exponent** $$t_r/\tau = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-9}}{2.5 \times 10^{-10}} = 18$$ $$e^{t_r/\tau} = e^{18} \approx 6.57 \times 10^{7}$$ **Step 3: Substitute into MTBF equation** $$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{5 \times 6.57 \times 10^{7}}{200 \times 10^{6} \times 50 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{3.28 \times 10^{8}}{10^{16}} = 3.28 \times 10^{-8} \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 4: Convert to years** $$\text{MTBF}_{years} = \dfrac{3.28 \times 10^{-8}}{3.15 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-15} \text{ years}$$ **Comment:** This MTBF is grossly inadequate — failure would occur many times per second. The synchronizer must be redesigned by **adding a third flip-flop** to extend the resolution window to $2T_{clk} - t_{su} = 9.5$ ns, or by **reducing the clock frequency** to allow longer resolution time. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the working of a two-flop synchronizer with a neat block diagram. Why is a single flip-flop synchronizer not recommended? [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Model Solution:** **Working of Two-Flop Synchronizer:** A two-flop synchronizer consists of two cascaded D flip-flops (FF1 and FF2), both clocked by the destination clock domain. The asynchronous input `async_in` is connected to the D input of FF1. On the first rising clock edge, FF1 samples the asynchronous data. This sampling may result in: 1. A clean capture (data settled well before the edge) → FF1 outputs a valid logic level. 2. A setup or hold time violation → FF1 enters metastability. The output of FF1 is connected to the D input of FF2. On the **second** rising clock edge (one full clock period later), FF2 samples the output of FF1. By this time, the metastability in FF1 has almost certainly resolved due to the regenerative action of its feedback loop. The output of FF2 is therefore a clean, valid logic level that can be safely consumed by the downstream synchronous logic. **Why Single Flip-Flop is Unsafe:** A single flip-flop has **zero resolution time margin** — the metastable output of the flip-flop is directly fed to the destination logic, which has no time to wait for resolution. This propagates the undefined state into the system, causing unpredictable behavior. A two-flop synchronizer provides a **full clock period of resolution time**, dramatically reducing the failure probability. **Part (b) [7 Marks]: List and briefly explain four techniques used to reduce the probability of metastability-induced failures in digital systems.** **Model Solution:** 1. **Use of Multi-Stage Synchronizers:** Cascading two or three flip-flops in series extends the available resolution time by $n \cdot T_{clk}$, where $n$ is the number of stages. Each additional stage multiplies MTBF by $e^{T_{clk}/\tau}$. 2. **Clock Frequency Reduction:** Lowering $f_{clk}$ directly increases $T_{clk}$, providing more resolution time. However, this trades off system performance. 3. **Use of Faster Flip-Flop Technology:** Flip-flops with higher gain-bandwidth products have smaller $\tau$ values, leading to faster metastability decay. Modern sub-nanometer CMOS processes have $\tau < 200$ ps. 4. **Handshake Protocol for Multi-bit CDC:** For multi-bit asynchronous buses, simple 2-flop synchronizers are insufficient because bits may arrive skewed. A request-acknowledge handshake protocol guarantees that all bits are stable before sampling, eliminating bit-skew-induced metastability. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Skipping units:** Always include seconds (s) in final answers — losing 0.5 mark if omitted. > 2. **Forgetting to convert to years:** When asked for MTBF in years, failing to divide by $3.15 \times 10^7$ loses 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing $f_{clk}$ with $f_{data}$:** $f_{clk}$ is the synchronizer sampling rate; $f_{data}$ is the asynchronous data transition rate. > 4. **Writing "metastability is avoided":** WRONG. The correct term is **"managed" or "mitigated."** Metastability is mathematically unavoidable. > 5. **Forgetting to draw the synchronizer block diagram:** In 7-mark questions, a diagram carries 1.5–2 marks. Always include one. > 6. **Not stating that synchronizers do NOT eliminate metastability:** A classic conceptual trap question. ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **Metastability** is a fundamental physical phenomenon in all bistable elements. - [ ] It occurs when **setup time or hold time** is violated. - [ ] The metastable output **decays exponentially** with time constant $\tau$. - [ ] **MTBF equation:** $\text{MTBF} = T_0 \cdot e^{t_r/\tau} / (f_{clk} \cdot f_{data})$. - [ ] **Two-flop synchronizer** is the standard CDC technique. - [ ] **Three-flop synchronizer** provides additional reliability for safety-critical systems. - [ ] **MTBF increases exponentially** with resolution time $t_r$. - [ ] **Resolution time** $t_r = T_{clk} - t_{su}$ for a 2-flop synchronizer. - [ ] **Multi-bit buses** require **handshake protocols**, not simple flip-flop synchronizers. - [ ] FPGA vendors provide built-in synchronizer primitives with documented MTBF. - [ ] **Handshake protocol** uses request-acknowledge to ensure data stability. - [ ] **Metastability aperture** is the small time window around the clock edge during which data transitions cause metastability. - [ ] **Device constant $T_0$** depends on fabrication technology and circuit topology. - [ ] **One year** = $3.15 \times 10^7$ seconds (conversion factor to remember). - [ ] **Setup time** = data stable BEFORE clock edge; **Hold time** = data stable AFTER clock edge. - [ ] **Synchronizers contain, do not eliminate** metastability. - [ ] **Faster flip-flops** have smaller $\tau$, giving better metastability performance. - [ ] **Clock domain crossing (CDC)** is the primary source of metastability in modern SoCs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** For numerical MTBF problems, always show: (1) unit conversion table, (2) exponent evaluation, (3) substitution, (4) final numerical answer with units, (5) engineering comment on adequacy. This structure consistently scores full marks.

topics.title: Data Synchronizer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT416 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Synchronizer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Data Synchronizer** is a sequential digital circuit block used to safely sample and transfer **asynchronous external input data** into a synchronous digital system operating on a stable internal clock domain. Its primary role is to resolve the **setup/hold time violations** that occur when an external signal transitions arbitrarily with respect to the receiving clock, thereby preventing the receiver flip-flop from entering the dangerous **metastable state**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Design of Logic Systems), the data synchronizer is classified under **Inter-System Communication and Reliability Design** of digital systems, sitting at the boundary between two clock domains — the **asynchronous data domain** and the **synchronous system clock domain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Examiner's Wording)**
> A *data synchronizer* is a hardware interface that allows an asynchronous input signal to be sampled by a clocked sequential element without producing metastability-related logic errors. The standard hardware implementation uses **N cascaded flip-flops (typically N = 2 or N = 3)** clocked by the destination (system) clock.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a **railway station platform** listening to announcements. The train arrives at unpredictable, irregular intervals (this is your **asynchronous data input**). However, your brain can only "process" information during the gaps between heartbeats (this is your **system clock**).

If a person shouts the train number *exactly* at the moment you blink, your brain might get a "garbled" or "frozen" reading — neither old nor new, just stuck in confusion. This frozen, ambiguous state is called **metastability**.

To avoid this confusion, you instinctively do the following:
1. **Ignore** the announcement instantly (**first flip-flop** absorbs the metastability).
2. **Wait one more cycle** to confirm the value (**second flip-flop** provides a clean, valid logic level).

This two-stage "listen–confirm" action is precisely how a **2-FF Data Synchronizer** operates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary**
> A data synchronizer is a "buffer zone" of one or more flip-flops that safely captures an externally arriving, unpredictable data signal into a clocked digital system.

### 1.3 Physical / Design Constants and Metrics

The following are standard, board-recognized metrics for synchronizer design:

- **Metastability Resolution Time Constant ($\tau$)** — technology-dependent; typically in the order of **picoseconds to nanoseconds**.
- **Allowed Resolution Time ($t_r$)** — the time budget between the first flip-flop output and the next system stage, equal to **one destination clock period minus setup time** ($t_{r} \approx T_{clk} - t_{su}$).
- **Standard Synchronizer Depth (N)** — commonly **N = 2** for moderate-speed buses and **N = 3** for high-reliability aerospace/military designs.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** — typically required to be **greater than 1 year** for industrial systems and **greater than 1000 years** for safety-critical systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Problem: Why Synchronization is Needed

In a purely synchronous system, every flip-flop receives its data input from a combinational logic block that has settled well before the active clock edge. However, when:

1. A **manual switch** is pressed by a human (true asynchronous behavior).
2. Data arrives from an **external bus, UART, sensor, or different clock domain**.
3. A signal passes through **asynchronous reset or power-on logic**.

…the data transition can occur **exactly at the sampling instant** of the destination flip-flop. This violates the **setup time ($t_{su}$)** or **hold time ($t_h$)** constraint.

### 2.2 Metastability — The Underlying Physics

When $t_{su}$ or $t_h$ is violated, the internal cross-coupled latch pair inside the flip-flop can settle into a **metastable equilibrium state** — neither logic 0 nor logic 1, but hovering at an intermediate voltage near $V_{DD}/2$.

- The metastable voltage decays exponentially as:

$$V(t) \approx V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

- The probability that the metastability has **not** resolved after time $t_r$ is:

$$P(t_{r}) = e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The probability of a metastability event is **never zero**, but it can be made astronomically small by giving the flip-flop enough resolution time and reducing its input transition rate.

### 2.3 Working Principle of an N-Stage Data Synchronizer

**Logic Flow (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1 — Asynchronous Input Arrival:** An external, unsynchronized signal `D_async` enters the first flip-flop (`FF1`). It may violate $t_{su}$/$t_h$ of `FF1`.
- **Step 2 — Metastability Capture:** `FF1` may enter metastability. Critically, **no downstream logic must sample `FF1` directly** during this time.
- **Step 3 — One Clock Cycle of Resolution:** After one destination clock period ($T_{clk}$), the metastable state has an overwhelming probability of resolving to a stable 0 or 1.
- **Step 4 — Second Stage Confirmation (`FF2`):** `FF2` samples `FF1`'s output. By this point, `FF1`'s output is stable, so `FF2` is guaranteed to capture a clean logic level.
- **Step 5 — Output Validity:** The output of `FF2` (`D_sync`) is presented to the rest of the synchronous system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Rule (KTU Board Emphasis):**
> The output of the **first** flip-flop must **NEVER** feed combinational logic that drives another flip-flop clocked on the **same edge**. The mandatory one-cycle wait for `FF2` is what saves the design.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Metastable Voltage Decay | $V(t) \approx V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Voltage vs. time during resolution |
| 2 | Probability of Unresolved Metastability | $P(t_{r}) = e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$ | Probability that metastability still exists after $t_r$ |
| 3 | Allowed Resolution Time | $t_{r} = T_{clk} - t_{su}$ | Time available for FF1 to resolve before FF2 samples |
| 4 | **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{t_{r}/\tau}}{T_{0} \cdot f_{D} \cdot f_{C}}$ | Average time between two metastability-induced system errors |
| 5 | Sampling Window | $T_{0} = \frac{t_{su} + t_{h}}{T_{clk}}$ | Vulnerable aperture for setup/hold violation |
| 6 | Synchronizer Latency | $L = N \cdot T_{clk}$ | Total pipeline delay (N stages × clock period) |
| 7 | Synchronizer Throughput | $\text{Th} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | One synchronized word per clock cycle |

Where:
- $f_{D}$ = asynchronous data transition frequency (Hz).
- $f_{C}$ = destination system clock frequency (Hz).
- $T_{0}$ = metastability window constant (technology-dependent).
- $\tau$ = resolution time constant (technology-dependent).
- $N$ = number of flip-flop stages in the synchronizer chain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation Notation Pitfall:** In KTU answer scripts, never use the vertical bar $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table — use $\lvert x \rvert$ in your LaTeX. Also, write $e^{t_r/\tau}$ carefully; board examiners mark the **exponent sign** strictly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor / Microcontroller Reset Inputs:** External `RESET` buttons are asynchronous; a 2-FF synchronizer is mandatory before this signal enters the CPU core.
- **UART / USB / I²C / SPI Bus Inputs:** Asynchronous serial data must be synchronized to the receiver's clock domain.
- **FPGA Designs with Multiple Clock Domains:** Xilinx and Altera/Intel application notes universally require 2-FF or 3-FF synchronizers across clock domain crossings (CDC).
- **Industrial PLC Systems:** Debounced switch inputs pass through synchronizers before being read by the scan logic.
- **Aerospace Avionics:** Triple-redundant synchronizers (N = 3) satisfy DO-254 safety certification levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Metastable Voltage Decay Expression

A flip-flop is internally a pair of cross-coupled CMOS inverters. During metastability, the storage node voltage is near the unstable equilibrium point.

**Step 1 — Write the differential equation for the small-signal deviation from equilibrium.**

For a cross-coupled inverter pair, the small-signal linearized differential equation is:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \cdot v(t)$$

This describes exponential decay of the voltage deviation from the metastable equilibrium point.

**Step 2 — Solve the first-order linear ODE.**

The general solution of $\frac{dv}{dt} = -\frac{v}{\tau}$ is:

$$v(t) = v(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Express the absolute node voltage (assuming initial voltage near $V_{DD}$).**

Adding the equilibrium voltage back:

$$V(t) \approx V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

> This is the **fundamental metastability decay equation**. The constant $\tau$ depends on transistor gain, capacitance, and process technology (typically 10 ps to 1 ns).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Probability of Unresolved Metastability

**Step 1 — Define the failure event.**

A "failure" is the event that after waiting time $t_r$, the metastable voltage has **not** decayed below the logic-threshold margin.

**Step 2 — Apply the exponential distribution.**

Empirically, the probability that the unresolved voltage still exceeds the threshold after time $t_r$ follows an exponential law:

$$P(t_{r}) = e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

A larger $t_r$ (slower clock or larger setup margin) exponentially reduces the failure probability. A smaller $\tau$ (faster, modern process) also helps, but introduces new challenges.

### 3.3 Full Derivation of the MTBF Equation

The MTBF equation is the **single most board-important derivation** for this topic. The KTU examiner expects students to derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the metastability event rate.**

Each time the asynchronous input toggles, there is one vulnerable window per clock cycle of the destination domain. The rate of vulnerable events is:

$$\text{Event Rate} = f_{D} \cdot f_{C} \cdot T_{0}$$

where $T_{0}$ is the metastability aperture constant (in seconds), representing the fraction of one clock period during which a setup/hold violation can occur.

**Step 2 — Compute the probability of an unresolved metastable event per occurrence.**

From Section 3.2:

$$P_{\text{unresolved}} = e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Combine event rate and per-event probability.**

The expected number of failures per second is the product of the event rate and the failure probability:

$$\text{Failures per Second} = (f_{D} \cdot f_{C} \cdot T_{0}) \cdot e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures).**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\text{Failures per Second}} = \frac{1}{f_{D} \cdot f_{C} \cdot T_{0} \cdot e^{-t_{r}/\tau}}$$

**Step 5 — Algebraically rearrange (move the exponential into the numerator):**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{t_{r}/\tau}}{T_{0} \cdot f_{D} \cdot f_{C}}$$

This is the **final, board-ready MTBF formula**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Valuation Tip:** When deriving MTBF, never skip the step "failures per second = event rate × per-event probability." Examiners award marks for showing this logic explicitly.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A digital system receives an asynchronous input at $f_{D} = 1 \text{ MHz}$. The system clock is $f_{C} = 50 \text{ MHz}$. The flip-flop has $T_{0} = 0.4 \text{ ns}$, $\tau = 50 \text{ ps}$, and $t_{su} = 1 \text{ ns}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the clock period.**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{C}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the allowed resolution time.**

$$t_{r} = T_{clk} - t_{su} = 20 \text{ ns} - 1 \text{ ns} = 19 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent.**

$$\frac{t_{r}}{\tau} = \frac{19 \times 10^{-9}}{50 \times 10^{-12}} = 380$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$e^{380} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{165}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator.**

$$T_{0} \cdot f_{D} \cdot f_{C} = (0.4 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (50 \times 10^{6}) = 20$$

**Step 6 — Compute MTBF.**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1.41 \times 10^{165}}{20} = 7.05 \times 10^{163} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to years for engineering intuition.**

$$\text{MTBF} \approx \frac{7.05 \times 10^{163}}{3.15 \times 10^{7}} \approx 2.24 \times 10^{156} \text{ years}$$

> This astronomically large MTBF confirms that a **2-FF synchronizer** is essentially "failsafe" for normal industrial applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 2-FF Data Synchronizer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ASYNC INPUT\nExternal Asynchronous Data"] --> B["FF1\nFirst Flip Flop\nMetastability Absorber"]
    C["SYSTEM CLOCK\nDestination Clock Domain"] --> B
    B --> D["META NODE\nResolution Window\n1 Clock Cycle"]
    D --> E["FF2\nSecond Flip Flop\nClean Output Latch"]
    C --> E
    E --> F["SYNC OUTPUT\nD sync to Internal Logic"]

    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 3-FF Synchronizer — High-Reliability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["ASYNC INPUT"] --> Q["FF1 STAGE\nMeta Capture"]
    CLK["DEST CLOCK"] --> Q
    Q --> R["FF2 STAGE\nFirst Confirm"]
    CLK --> R
    R --> S["FF3 STAGE\nSecond Confirm"]
    CLK --> S
    S --> T["SYNC OUTPUT\nTriple Clean"]

    style P fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style Q fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style R fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style S fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style T fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style CLK fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Timing Diagram — Synchronizer Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Async Input
    participant F1 as FF1 Output
    participant F2 as FF2 Output
    participant C as System Clock

    Note over A,F2: Two Stage Data Synchronization Timeline
    A->>A: External data transitions at unpredictable time
    C->>F1: Rising edge 1 - FF1 samples async input
    F1->>F1: Possible metastability window begins
    Note over F1: Resolution occurs within Tclk
    C->>F2: Rising edge 2 - FF2 samples clean FF1
    F2->>F2: Clean synchronized output available
    C->>F1: Rising edge 3 - Pipeline ready for next input
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Synchronizer Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Asynchronous Data Arrives"] --> CHECK{"Setup Hold\nViolation?"}
    CHECK -- YES --> FF1["FF1 Absorbs\nMetastable Event"]
    CHECK -- NO --> FF1
    FF1 --> WAIT["Wait One Clock\nPeriod Tclk"]
    WAIT --> FF2["FF2 Captures\nClean Logic Level"]
    FF2 --> VALIDATE{"Output\nStable?"}
    VALIDATE -- YES --> OUTPUT["Synchronized Data\nto Internal Logic"]
    VALIDATE -- NO --> FF3["FF3 Final Stage\n3 FF Synchronizer"]
    FF3 --> OUTPUT

    style START fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style CHECK fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style FF1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style WAIT fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style FF2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style VALIDATE fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style FF3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style OUTPUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The pink nodes represent "hazard-prone" metastability regions. The blue nodes represent clean, stable, synchronous zones. The green nodes are final safe outputs. The lavender node is the mandatory resolution wait window.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define metastability in a digital flip-flop. Why is it a problem in asynchronous data transfer?**

**Model Answer:**

Metastability is the abnormal, indeterminate output state of a bistable element (flip-flop) when its setup time ($t_{su}$) or hold time ($t_h$) is violated. The flip-flop's output voltage hovers at an intermediate level near $V_{DD}/2$ instead of cleanly resolving to logic 0 or logic 1.

It is problematic in asynchronous data transfer because:

1. The output is neither valid 0 nor valid 1, causing downstream gates to behave unpredictably.
2. The metastable state can persist long enough to be sampled by the next sequential stage, propagating the error throughout the system.
3. The resolution time is statistical, making it impossible to guarantee correct operation without a synchronizer.

*(Self-words: 3-mark answer — definition 1 mark, two consequences 1.5 marks, conclusion 0.5 mark)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the purpose of a data synchronizer? Why is a 2-stage flip-flop synchronizer preferred over a single flip-flop?**

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose:** A data synchronizer safely transfers an asynchronous external input signal into a synchronous clocked domain, preventing metastability from propagating into the system's internal logic.

**Why 2-FF over 1-FF:**

1. A single flip-flop **cannot guarantee** that metastability has resolved within the available clock period; its output may still be oscillating when the next stage samples it.
2. The **second flip-flop provides a full clock period** for the first flip-flop to statistically resolve its metastability, dramatically improving MTBF.
3. The second stage guarantees a clean, stable logic level for downstream synchronous logic.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to subtract $t_{su}$ when computing $t_r$. Always write $t_r = T_{clk} - t_{su}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Mixing up $f_D$ (data toggle rate) with $f_C$ (clock frequency) in the MTBF formula. **Data goes first, clock second** in the denominator: $T_0 \cdot f_D \cdot f_C$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Stating "MTBF = e^(t/τ)" alone. The full equation includes $T_0$, $f_D$, $f_C$ — partial formulas lose 4 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing the metastability window constant $T_0$ with the clock period $T_{clk}$. They are different.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Drawing a single FF and claiming "this is a synchronizer." Examiners specifically look for **N ≥ 2 stages**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **two-stage data synchronizer** used for asynchronous input signals. Discuss how metastability is handled in this circuit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **MTBF equation** for a data synchronizer and explain how the MTBF can be improved by design choices. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-Stage Data Synchronizer — Block Diagram and Operation:**

Refer to the architecture in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Working Explanation:**

- **Stage 1 — FF1 (Metastability Absorber):** The asynchronous external input `D_async` is fed to the D input of FF1. FF1 is clocked by the destination system clock. If `D_async` transitions within the setup/hold window of the system clock, FF1 may enter metastability. However, no logic downstream of FF1 samples it during the same clock edge.
- **Wait Period — One Clock Cycle:** The system waits for one full clock period $T_{clk}$. During this interval, FF1's metastable voltage decays exponentially per $V(t) \approx V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$. With overwhelming probability, FF1 resolves to a valid 0 or 1.
- **Stage 2 — FF2 (Clean Output Latch):** At the next rising clock edge, FF2 samples FF1's now-stable output. Since FF2's setup/hold window is respected (FF1 has already settled), FF2 captures a guaranteed-clean logic level.
- **Output:** The output of FF2 is the synchronized data `D_sync`, safe for use by the rest of the synchronous system.

**Key Design Insight:** The latency of the synchronizer is exactly **2 clock cycles**. The throughput is **1 synchronized data per clock cycle**.

*(Mark split: Diagram 2 marks, Stage 1 explanation 1.5 marks, Wait period 1 mark, Stage 2 explanation 1.5 marks, Summary 1 mark)*

**(b) Derivation of MTBF and Design Improvements:**

**Step 1 — Define the metastability event rate.**
Each asynchronous transition has one vulnerable window per system clock cycle:
$$\text{Event Rate} = f_{D} \cdot f_{C} \cdot T_{0}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of unresolved metastability per event.**
$$P_{\text{unresolved}} = e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Failures per second.**
$$\lambda = (f_{D} \cdot f_{C} \cdot T_{0}) \cdot e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to get MTBF.**
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{e^{t_{r}/\tau}}{T_{0} \cdot f_{D} \cdot f_{C}}$$

**Design Improvements to Increase MTBF:**

1. **Increase $t_r$:** Use a slower destination clock (larger $T_{clk}$) — but this reduces system performance.
2. **Add more flip-flop stages (N = 3):** Each additional stage exponentially increases effective resolution time.
3. **Use faster flip-flop technology (smaller $\tau$):** Modern sub-nanometer CMOS has smaller $\tau$.
4. **Reduce asynchronous toggle rate $f_D$:** Add input debouncing or conditioning circuitry.
5. **Use synchronizer flip-flops with Schmitt-trigger inputs:** Improves noise immunity.

*(Mark split: Event rate derivation 2 marks, unresolved probability 1 mark, full MTBF equation 2 marks, 4 design improvements 2 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **metastability** in flip-flops. Derive the expression for **voltage decay** during metastability resolution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A digital system operates with a **50 MHz destination clock**. An asynchronous input toggles at **2 MHz**. The flip-flop parameters are $T_{0} = 0.5 \text{ ns}$, $\tau = 30 \text{ ps}$, and $t_{su} = 2 \text{ ns}$. Calculate the **MTBF** of a 2-FF data synchronizer. Also calculate the MTBF if the clock frequency is reduced to **25 MHz**. Comment on the results. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Metastability Concept and Voltage Decay Derivation:**

**Concept:** A flip-flop is internally a pair of cross-coupled inverters with two stable equilibrium points (logic 0 and logic 1). When the data input transitions exactly within the setup/hold aperture of the active clock edge, the feedback loop is balanced. The latch may temporarily settle at the **unstable metastable equilibrium** between the two valid logic levels.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Small-signal model:** Near metastability, the cross-coupled inverter pair behaves as a linear amplifier with negative feedback. The deviation from the metastable equilibrium follows the ODE:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = -\frac{v(t)}{\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the ODE:**
$$v(t) = v(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Express absolute node voltage:**
$$V(t) \approx V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is the resolution time constant, technology-dependent (typically 10 ps – 1 ns).

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The metastable voltage decays exponentially. After time $t_r$, the probability of unresolved metastability is:

$$P(t_{r}) = e^{-t_{r}/\tau}$$

*(Mark split: Concept 2 marks, ODE 2 marks, solution 2 marks, probability 1 mark)*

**(b) MTBF Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $T_{clk}$ for 50 MHz system:**
$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $t_r$ (50 MHz case):**
$$t_{r} = T_{clk} - t_{su} = 20 - 2 = 18 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate exponent (50 MHz case):**
$$\frac{t_{r}}{\tau} = \frac{18 \times 10^{-9}}{30 \times 10^{-12}} = 600$$

**Step 4 — Calculate numerator (50 MHz case):**
$$e^{600} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{260}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate denominator (50 MHz case):**
$$T_{0} \cdot f_{D} \cdot f_{C} = (0.5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (50 \times 10^{6}) = 50$$

**Step 6 — MTBF (50 MHz case):**
$$\text{MTBF}_{50} = \frac{1.39 \times 10^{260}}{50} = 2.78 \times 10^{258} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7 — Repeat for 25 MHz case:**
$$T_{clk} = 40 \text{ ns}, \quad t_{r} = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ ns}$$
$$\frac{t_{r}}{\tau} = \frac{38 \times 10^{-9}}{30 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1266.67$$
$$e^{1266.67} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{550}$$
$$T_{0} \cdot f_{D} \cdot f_{C} = (0.5 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{6}) \cdot (25 \times 10^{6}) = 25$$
$$\text{MTBF}_{25} = \frac{1.07 \times 10^{550}}{25} = 4.28 \times 10^{548} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 8 — Comment on the Results:**

The MTBF at **25 MHz** is approximately **$10^{290}$ times larger** than at 50 MHz. This demonstrates that halving the clock frequency produces an **astronomically exponential improvement** in reliability, because the exponent $t_r/\tau$ doubles.

In engineering practice, this means slowing down the destination clock is the **single most effective design lever** for improving synchronizer MTBF, at the cost of reduced system throughput.

*(Mark split: Six calculation steps × 0.75 mark = 4.5 marks, comment 1.5 marks, MTBF formula used correctly 1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Data Synchronizer = N cascaded flip-flops** at the boundary between an asynchronous source and a synchronous system clock.
- **Standard depths:** N = 2 (general purpose), N = 3 (high-reliability / aerospace).
- **Metastability** is a probabilistic event, never zero, but exponentially reducible.
- **Voltage decay formula:** $V(t) \approx V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$.
- **Unresolved probability:** $P(t_r) = e^{-t_r/\tau}$.
- **MTBF Master Formula:** $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{t_r/\tau}}{T_0 \cdot f_D \cdot f_C}$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Resolution time:** $t_r = T_{clk} - t_{su}$ — never forget to subtract setup time.
- **Synchronizer latency** = $N \times T_{clk}$ cycles.
- **Synchronizer throughput** = $\dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ (one synchronized word per clock).
- **Critical design rule:** Output of the **first FF** must never feed combinational logic — only the **second FF's output** is safe.
- **Most effective MTBF improvements (in priority order):** (1) Slower destination clock, (2) More FF stages, (3) Faster flip-flop technology, (4) Lower async toggle rate, (5) Better input conditioning.
- **Real-world examples:** External reset buttons, UART receivers, FPGA clock domain crossings, PLC debounced switch inputs, aerospace triple-redundant synchronizers.
- **Board exam golden rule:** Always write the **full MTBF equation**, never abbreviate it; examiners award 2 marks just for the correct formula statement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timing Hazard - Static Hazard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT416 - DESIGN OF LOGIC SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Timing Hazard - Static Hazard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In asynchronous combinational logic circuits, signals may travel through different paths having different propagation delays. When a single input variable changes (transition), the output of the circuit may momentarily produce an **unwanted glitch or spike** even when the Boolean expression does not predict any change. This erroneous transient behavior of the output is called a **Timing Hazard** (or simply a **Logic Hazard**).

A **Static Hazard** is a specific category of timing hazard where the output of a combinational logic circuit is **nominally supposed to remain at a constant logic level** (either constantly `0` or constantly `1`) during a single input variable transition, but **momentarily deviates to the opposite logic level** due to unequal propagation delays along different signal paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Hazard Definition (Examination-Ready Statement):**
> A *static hazard* occurs in a combinational network when, during a single input change, the output momentarily changes from its expected steady-state value and then returns to the original value. If the expected level is `1`, it is called a **Static-1 Hazard**. If the expected level is `0`, it is called a **Static-0 Hazard**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Signal Intuition

Imagine two parallel roads merging onto a single lane. Two sensors (analogous to AND/OR gates) detect the approaching cars (input signals `A` and `B`). The traffic light (output `F`) is supposed to stay **GREEN** (logic `1`) as long as at least one car is approaching. However, suppose the first sensor's signal arrives **slightly late** (propagation delay `t_pd1 = 10 ns`) and the second sensor's signal arrives **earlier** (`t_pd2 = 5 ns`). For a brief moment — say, when one car exits and the other is just entering — **both signals may momentarily report "no car present"**, causing the light to flicker **RED** (logic `0`) before turning GREEN again. This brief, unwanted flicker is exactly a **Static-1 Hazard**.

Similarly, a **Static-0 Hazard** is like a light that is supposed to stay **RED** but flickers **GREEN** momentarily due to a sensor reading delay mismatch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Hazards are **purely dynamic, physical phenomena** caused by real-world gate propagation delays. From a **pure Boolean (algebraic)** perspective, the output is *supposed* to remain constant. The hazard is a violation of that ideal Boolean expectation.

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Static Hazards

| Hazard Type | Expected Output Level | Spurious Glitch Direction | Most Common In |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Static-1 Hazard** | 1 (HIGH) | Glitch falls to 0 (LOW) | Sum-of-Products (SOP) circuits |
| **Static-0 Hazard** | 0 (LOW) | Glitch rises to 1 (HIGH) | Product-of-Sums (POS) circuits |

## 1.4 Physical Constant & Standard Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glitch Duration Metric:** The width of a static hazard glitch is governed by the **propagation delay difference** between the two reconvergent signal paths in the circuit. The glitch width is approximately:
> $$\Delta t_{glitch} \approx \vert t_{pd,\text{path}_1} - t_{pd,\text{path}_2} \vert$$
> where $t_{pd}$ is measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** in standard CMOS logic families. The glitch width is **NOT** dependent on the input frequency; it is purely a function of **asymmetric gate delays**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Root Cause — The Algebraic Condition for Hazards

A static hazard occurs in a combinational circuit when **a single input variable $x$ and its complement $x'$ both appear as product terms (in SOP) or sum terms (in POS) in the minimized expression** without proper redundant cover.

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Origin of Static-1 Hazard

Consider a two-level SOP expression:
$$F = AB + A'C$$

When $B = C = 1$, the function reduces to:
$$F = A + A' = 1 \quad \text{(Boolean ideal)}$$

However, in a real gate-level circuit:
- The `AND` gate for `AB` must wait for `A` to propagate.
- The `AND` gate for `A'C` must wait for the **inverted** `A` (after passing through a NOT gate) and `C` to propagate.

When `A` transitions from `1` to `0`:
- The `AB` term **collapses to 0** almost immediately (since `A` falls).
- The `A'C` term takes an **extra gate delay** (the inverter's $t_{pd}$) to become `1`.

For that brief moment, **both product terms equal 0**, so the OR gate's output briefly drops to `0`, producing a **Static-1 hazard** — a glitch on a circuit that should remain at `1`.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Origin of Static-0 Hazard

By De Morgan duality, a Static-0 hazard occurs in POS expressions when **a variable and its complement both appear in sum terms without redundant cover**:
$$F = (A + B)(A' + C)$$

## 2.2 The Karnaugh Map Diagnostic Criterion

A **static hazard exists** in a two-level SOP realization if and only if the Karnaugh map has **two adjacent 1-cells that are NOT covered by a common prime implicant**. Similarly, for POS, a static-0 hazard exists if **two adjacent 0-cells are not covered by a common prime implicate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Adjacent Cells Matter:** Two adjacent cells in a K-map differ in exactly **one variable**. This means the two minterms they represent correspond to two paths through the circuit that differ only in one literal. The hazard occurs during the transition between these two minterms.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Algorithm for Hazard Detection & Elimination (SOP)

The systematic procedure followed in KTU board examinations is:

**Step 1:** Express the function in **canonical SOP** (sum of minterms) form.

**Step 2:** Draw the **Karnaugh Map** and identify all prime implicants.

**Step 3:** Identify the **minimum cover** (essential prime implicants only). This minimized SOP is the **hazard-prone** form.

**Step 4:** Examine every pair of **adjacent 1-cells**. If any pair is **not bridged by a single prime implicant** in the minimum cover, that pair contributes a static-1 hazard.

**Step 5:** Add the **redundant (non-essential) prime implicant** that covers the hazardous pair. The resulting expression is **hazard-free**.

**Step 6:** Verify by drawing the **static-0 complement K-map** (or by duality) that no static-0 hazard was introduced.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Boolean Ideal Output (Static-1) | $F = x + x' = 1$ | Algebraic identity |
| 2 | Glitch Width (Real Gate) | $\Delta t_{glitch} \approx \vert t_{pd,1} - t_{pd,2} \vert$ | nanoseconds (ns) |
| 3 | Hazard Indicator (SOP) | Adjacent 1-cells not covered by common prime implicant | K-map test |
| 4 | Hazard Indicator (POS) | Adjacent 0-cells not covered by common prime implicate | K-map test |
| 5 | Hazard-Free SOP | $F = \sum \text{(all prime implicants covering all minterm pairs)}$ | Includes redundant PI |
| 6 | De Morgan Duality | $F_{hazard\text{-}free,SOP} \leftrightarrow \overline{F}_{hazard\text{-}free,POS}$ | Logical equivalence |
| 7 | Consensus Term | $XY + X'Z + YZ = XY + X'Z$ | $YZ$ is the consensus of $XY$ and $X'Z$ |
| 8 | Number of Adjacent Pairs to Check | $P = \binom{n_1}{2}$ | where $n_1$ = number of 1-cells |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Hazard-free design is **mandatory** in asynchronous sequential circuits, edge-triggered flip-flop inputs (clock, preset, clear), and high-reliability systems such as **aerospace flight control**, **medical device controllers**, and **industrial safety interlocks**. Failure to eliminate hazards can cause catastrophic data corruption or unintended state transitions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Identifying and Eliminating a Static-1 Hazard

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Design a **hazard-free** two-level AND-OR (SOP) realization of the Boolean function:
$$F(A, B, C, D) = \sum m(0, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, 12, 14, 15)$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Construct the Karnaugh Map

We map the 12 minterms onto a 4-variable K-map (AB on rows, CD on columns):

| AB \ CD | 00 | 01 | 11 | 10 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **00** | 1 (m0) | 0 | 1 (m3) | 1 (m2) |
| **01** | 1 (m4) | 0 | 1 (m7) | 1 (m6) |
| **11** | 1 (m12) | 0 | 1 (m15) | 1 (m14) |
| **10** | 1 (m8) | 0 | 1 (m11) | 1 (m10) |

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Identify All Prime Implicants

We list every possible prime implicant grouping:

- **PI₁** = $\overline{A} \cdot \overline{D}$ (covers m0, m2, m4, m6, m8, m10)
- **PI₂** = $C$ (covers m3, m7, m15, m11)
- **PI₃** = $B$ (covers m6, m7, m14, m15)
- **PI₄** = $A \cdot \overline{D}$ (covers m8, m10, m12, m14) — *non-essential*
- **PI₅** = $\overline{A} \cdot C$ (covers m3, m7) — *non-essential, redundant*

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Determine the Minimum Cover (Essential Prime Implicants)

An essential prime implicant contains a minterm that is covered by no other prime implicant.

- **m0** is only covered by PI₁ $\Rightarrow$ **PI₁ is essential**
- **m3** is only covered by PI₂ (or PI₅) $\Rightarrow$ **PI₂ is essential**
- **m7** is only covered by PI₂ and PI₃ $\Rightarrow$ **PI₃ is essential**

So the **minimum cover** is:
$$F_{min} = \overline{A}\overline{D} + C + B = \overline{A}\overline{D} + B + C$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Detect Static-1 Hazards

We now check every pair of adjacent 1-cells **not bridged** by a single prime implicant in the minimum cover.

Consider the pair **(m11, m15)**: These cells are adjacent (differ in variable `A` only). In the K-map:
- m11 (row 10, col 11): covered by PI₂ ($C$) only — since $A=1, B=0, C=1, D=1$.
- m15 (row 11, col 11): covered by PI₂ ($C$) and PI₃ ($B$).

This pair **IS** covered by PI₂, so **no hazard** here.

Consider the pair **(m4, m12)**: These cells are adjacent (differ in variable `B` only).
- m4 (row 01, col 00): covered by PI₁ ($\overline{A}\overline{D}$) only — $A=0, B=1, C=0, D=0$.
- m12 (row 11, col 00): covered by PI₁ ($\overline{A}\overline{D}$)? Wait, $A=1$, so $\overline{A}=0$ and PI₁ does NOT cover m12.

Let us recheck: PI₁ is $\overline{A}\overline{D}$. For m12, $A=1$, so $\overline{A}=0$, meaning **m12 is NOT covered by PI₁**.

The prime implicant that covers m12 in the original list is **PI₄ = $A\overline{D}$**. Since PI₄ is **non-essential** in the minimum cover, the adjacent pair (m4, m12) is **NOT bridged** by a common essential prime implicant. This is a **Static-1 Hazard candidate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When `B` transitions while `A=1, C=0, D=0`:
> - Path 1: $B=1 \Rightarrow$ F = $\overline{A}\overline{D} + C + B = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1$
> - Path 2: $B=0 \Rightarrow$ F = $\overline{A}\overline{D} + C + B = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$ (if no other PI covers it)
>
> A glitch occurs as $B$ transitions.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Add the Redundant Prime Implicant

To eliminate the hazard between m4 and m12, we add **PI₄ = $A\overline{D}$** as a redundant cover term:

$$F_{hazard\text{-}free} = \overline{A}\overline{D} + B + C + A\overline{D}$$

We can simplify the first and last terms using the **consensus theorem** (or just observe they are both $\overline{D}$ terms):
$$F_{hazard\text{-}free} = \overline{D}(\overline{A} + A) + B + C = \overline{D} + B + C$$

> [!NOTE]
> The hazard-free form of this function is simply $F = B + C + \overline{D}$. The original minimum cover $F_{min} = B + C + \overline{A}\overline{D}$ would suffer from a Static-1 hazard during the $B$-transition on $A=1, C=0, D=0$ — specifically the transition between m12 and m4.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Verify by Simulating the Hazard

Consider input change: $(A, B, C, D) = (1, 0, 0, 0) \rightarrow (1, 1, 0, 0)$.

| Expression | F at start (B=0) | F at end (B=1) | Behavior |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $F_{min} = \overline{A}\overline{D} + B + C$ | $\overline{1}\cdot\overline{0} + 0 + 0 = 0$ | $\overline{1}\cdot\overline{0} + 1 + 0 = 1$ | Glitch-free (F goes 0→1, not static) |
| $F_{min} = \overline{A}\overline{D} + B + C$ at $(1,1,0,0) \rightarrow (1,0,0,0)$ | $1$ | $0$ | Glitch-free |
| Consider $(A, B, C, D)$ going from $(0,1,0,0) \rightarrow (1,1,0,0)$ | $\overline{0}\cdot\overline{0}+1+0=1$ | $\overline{1}\cdot\overline{0}+1+0=1$ | Should stay 1 |
| But during transition (A has flipped, B has not yet due to delay): $A=0$ from old, $B$ held at 1 momentarily, then $A=1$ arrives | $\overline{0}\cdot\overline{0}+1+0=1$ | momentarily $\overline{1}\cdot\overline{0}+1+0=1$ | OK so far |
| Now B changes: $B=0, A=1$: $\overline{1}\cdot\overline{0}+0+0=0$ **GLITCH!** | Static-1 hazard! |

The glitch is eliminated in $F_{hazard\text{-}free} = B + C + \overline{D}$ because the redundant $\overline{D}$ term keeps the output at `1` when $B$ falls and $\overline{A}\overline{D}$ would have temporarily turned off.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Static-0 Hazard in POS Realization

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Examine the function $F(A, B, C) = (A + B)(\overline{A} + C)$ for static-0 hazards.

### 3.2.2 Algebraic Analysis

When $B = 0$ and $C = 0$, the function reduces to:
$$F = A \cdot \overline{A} = 0 \quad \text{(Boolean ideal)}$$

However, physically:
- Sum term $(A+B)$ produces a 0 only when $A=0$ AND $B=0$ (delayed path through OR gate).
- Sum term $(\overline{A}+C)$ produces a 0 only when $A=1$ AND $C=0$ (delayed path through OR + NOT).

During the transition $A: 1 \to 0$ with $B=0, C=0$:
- $(A+B)$: was `0` when $A=0$ (already), but transitions from `1` to `0` as $A$ falls.
- $(\overline{A}+C)$: was `0` when $A=1$, transitions to `1` as $A$ falls. **But there is an inverter delay!**

For the brief inverter delay, $A$ has become `0` but $\overline{A}$ is still showing `1` (or vice versa). Both sum terms can momentarily evaluate to `0`, making $F$ spike to `1` — a **Static-0 hazard**.

### 3.2.3 K-Map Identification

Plot the function in POS form. Zeros occur at:
- $(A+B)$ sum term $= 0$ when $A=0, B=0$ (covers minterm $\overline{A}\overline{B}$ = row 0, col 0 in 2-var)
- $(\overline{A}+C)$ sum term $= 0$ when $A=1, C=0$ (covers minterm $A\overline{C}$ = row 1, col 0 in 2-var)

The 0-cells in the K-map are **adjacent** (they differ only in $A$). The minimum POS cover consists of exactly these two sum terms, which do **not share a common implicate**. Therefore, a **Static-0 hazard** exists.

### 3.2.4 Elimination

Add the redundant sum term $(B+C)$ which covers the consensus of the two original terms:
$$F_{hazard\text{-}free} = (A + B)(\overline{A} + C)(B + C)$$

This corresponds to the consensus theorem: $XY + X'Z + YZ = XY + X'Z$ (POS dual).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Static-1 Hazard | Static-0 Hazard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Expected Output** | 1 (HIGH) | 0 (LOW) |
| **Glitch Direction** | 1 → 0 → 1 | 0 → 1 → 0 |
| **Common Realization Form** | Two-level AND-OR (SOP) | Two-level OR-AND (POS) |
| **K-Map Signature** | Two adjacent 1-cells uncovered by common prime implicant | Two adjacent 0-cells uncovered by common prime implicate |
| **Hazard-Free Cover** | Add redundant prime implicant (consensus term) | Add redundant sum term (dual consensus) |
| **Algebraic Trigger** | $F = x_1 x_2 + x_1' x_3$ pattern with $x_2 = x_3 = 1$ | $F = (x_1 + x_2)(x_1' + x_3)$ pattern with $x_2 = x_3 = 0$ |
| **Detection Test** | Static-1 K-map (function map) | Static-0 K-map (complement map) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hazard Formation in SOP Circuit

The diagram below illustrates the gate-level topology that produces a Static-1 hazard. Note how the input `A` is split into two paths (direct and through an inverter), each feeding a different AND gate, and the AND outputs converge at an OR gate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input A] --> INV[NOT Gate]
    A --> AND1[AND Gate 1]
    INV[NOT Gate] --> AND2[AND Gate 2]
    B[Input B] --> AND1
    C[Input C] --> AND2
    AND1[AND Gate 1] --> OR[OR Gate]
    AND2[AND Gate 2] --> OR
    OR[OR Gate] --> F[Output F]
    A[Input A] -.transition.-> P1[Path 1: A direct]
    A[Input A] -.transition.-> P2[Path 2: A through NOT]
    P1[Path 1] -.faster.-> AND1
    P2[Path 2] -.slower.-> AND2
    AND1 -.collapses to 0.-> P3[AND1 Output Glitch]
    AND2 -.delayed rise to 1.-> P4[AND2 Output Glitch]
    P3 --> OR
    P4 --> OR
    OR --> GLITCH[Static-1 Glitch at F]
    classDef inputNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000
    classDef gateNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    classDef outputNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    classDef hazardNode fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    class A,B,C inputNode
    class INV,AND1,AND2,OR gateNode
    class F outputNode
    class GLITCH hazardNode
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hazard Elimination by Consensus Term

The diagram below shows how adding a redundant consensus-term AND gate (highlighted in green) eliminates the Static-1 hazard by maintaining the OR output at logic `1` during the dangerous transition window.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ORIGINAL["Original Hazard-Prone Circuit"]
        A1[Input A] --> INV1[NOT Gate]
        A1 --> AND1A[AND1: A and B]
        B1[Input B] --> AND1A
        INV1 --> AND2A[AND2: NOT-A and C]
        C1[Input C] --> AND2A
        AND1A --> OR1[OR Gate]
        AND2A --> OR1
        OR1 --> F1[Output F: HAZARD]
    end
    subgraph REDUNDANT["Added Consensus Cover"]
        B2[Input B] --> AND3[AND3: B and C - REDUNDANT]
        C2[Input C] --> AND3
        AND3 --> OR1
    end
    classDef hazardBox fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    classDef safeBox fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    classDef gateBox fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    class F1 hazardBox
    class AND3 safeBox
    class INV1,AND1A,AND2A,OR1,AND1 gateBox
```

## 4.3 Timing Diagram of a Static-1 Hazard

The waveform diagram below shows the temporal evolution of inputs and the output glitch. The output `F` is expected to remain at `1` throughout the `A` transition, but it momentarily dips to `0` for a duration equal to the inverter propagation delay.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Input A
    participant NA as NOT A
    participant AB as AND1 Output AB
    participant NAC as AND2 Output NAC
    participant F as Final Output F
    Note over A,F: Initial state: A=1, B=1, C=1
    A->>A: A transitions 1 to 0 at t=0
    NA->>NA: NOT A transitions 0 to 1 at t=t_pd_inverter
    AB->>AB: AB collapses from 1 to 0 at t=0
    NAC->>NAC: NAC rises from 0 to 1 at t=t_pd_inverter
    F->>F: F is 1 at t=0
    F->>F: F glitches to 0 during 0 less than t less than t_pd_inverter
    F->>F: F returns to 1 at t=t_pd_inverter
    Note over F: STATIC-1 HAZARD: Glitch width approx t_pd_inverter
```

## 4.4 K-Map Schematic — Hazard Detection Visualization

The K-map below marks the prime implicants, the bridged cell pairs (safe), and the unbridged cell pair (hazardous).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KMAP["4-Variable Karnaugh Map for F = sum m 0,2,3,4,6,7,8,10,11,12,14,15"]
        direction TB
        R0["Row 00: m0=1, m1=0, m3=1, m2=1"]
        R1["Row 01: m4=1, m5=0, m7=1, m6=1"]
        R2["Row 11: m12=1, m13=0, m15=1, m14=1"]
        R3["Row 10: m8=1, m9=0, m11=1, m10=1"]
    end
    subgraph COVER["Prime Implicant Cover"]
        PI1["PI1: A-bar D-bar - ESSENTIAL"]
        PI2["PI2: C - ESSENTIAL"]
        PI3["PI3: B - ESSENTIAL"]
        PI4["PI4: A D-bar - REDUNDANT needed for hazard-free"]
    end
    subgraph BRIDGED["Adjacent Pairs Bridged (SAFE)"]
        SAFE1["m2-m3: covered by PI2"]
        SAFE2["m6-m7: covered by PI2 and PI3"]
        SAFE3["m10-m11: covered by PI2"]
    end
    subgraph UNBRIDGED["Adjacent Pair UNBRIDGED (HAZARD)"]
        HAZ1["m4-m12: only PI1 covers m4, only PI4 covers m12"]
        HAZ2["Hazardous when B transitions at A=1, C=0, D=0"]
    end
    classDef safeBox fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    classDef hazardBox fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    classDef coverBox fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    class PI1,PI2,PI3,PI4 coverBox
    class SAFE1,SAFE2,SAFE3 safeBox
    class HAZ1,HAZ2 hazardBox
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Static-1 and Static-0 Hazards
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Static-1 Hazard** is a glitch in a combinational logic circuit in which the output is expected to remain at logic `1` during a single input variable transition, but momentarily falls to `0` due to unequal propagation delays through reconvergent signal paths.

A **Static-0 Hazard** is the dual case: the output is expected to remain at logic `0` during a single input transition, but momentarily rises to `1` due to gate delay mismatches.

Static-1 hazards are typical of two-level AND-OR (SOP) realizations, while Static-0 hazards are typical of two-level OR-AND (POS) realizations. The K-map signature for Static-1 is two adjacent `1`-cells not covered by a common prime implicant; for Static-0, it is two adjacent `0`-cells not covered by a common prime implicate.

---

### Question 2: How are Static Hazards Eliminated in Combinational Circuits?
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Static hazards are eliminated by ensuring that **every pair of adjacent minterms (for Static-1) or adjacent maxterms (for Static-0) in the Karnaugh map is covered by a common prime implicant (or implicate) in the implementation**.

For SOP realizations, this is achieved by adding **redundant consensus prime implicants** that bridge the hazardous adjacent 1-cell pairs. Algebraically, this corresponds to the consensus theorem: $XY + X'Z + YZ = XY + X'Z$, where the addition of $YZ$ does not change the function's truth table but eliminates the hazard by providing a continuous cover during the dangerous transition.
